results.question: How did Romulus die?
results.ideal_answer: The sources disagree. The official version, in both Livy and Plutarch, is that while Romulus was reviewing the army at the Goat's Marsh a sudden storm enveloped him and he was carried up to heaven, thereafter worshipped as the god Quirinus. But both historians also preserve a darker rumor: that the senators (fathers), resentful of his rule, fell upon him and tore his body to pieces, each carrying away a part — Plutarch placing the deed in the temple of Vulcan.
results.answer:
The sources offer several accounts of Romulus's death, all agreeing he vanished suddenly and was never seen again, but differing on the cause.

According to Livy, while Romulus was holding an assembly to review his army in the plain near the lake of Capra, a sudden storm with great thunder and lightning arose, and a dense mist enveloped him so that he was taken from sight. He was never seen on earth again. The senators standing nearest claimed he had been carried aloft by the storm; the people then hailed him as a god. Livy adds, however, that even then some silently believed the king had been torn to pieces by the hands of the senators — though this rumour was not widely credited. Further belief in his deification was bolstered by Proculus Julius, who reported that Romulus descended from heaven, appeared to him, and declared that Rome would become the capital of the world [7].

Plutarch provides additional versions. He writes that some fancied the senators fell upon Romulus in the temple of Vulcan, cut his body into pieces, and each carried away a part concealed in his bosom. Another account places the event at the Goat's Marsh, where Romulus was haranguing the people outside the city; strange disorders in the air, darkness, thunder, and boisterous winds scattered the people while the senators remained together. When light returned, the king was missing; the senators forbade any search and instructed the people to worship him as a god taken up to heaven. Plutarch also records that Proculus took an oath that he saw Romulus ascend to heaven in his arms and vestments, crying out that he should be called Quirinus [2][9].

In summary, the traditions fall into two main strands: (1) Romulus was murdered and dismembered by the patricians, who then concealed the crime; (2) he was taken up to heaven in a divine storm and became the god Quirinus. Both Livy and Plutarch present these versions side by side, noting the suspicion of senatorial foul play while also recounting the official story of apotheosis.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided source passages, with no invented or unsupported content. | completeness: The answer comprehensively covers both the official tradition of Romulus's apotheosis in a storm and the darker rumor of his murder and dismemberment by the senators, drawing on both Livy and Plutarch and including all key details the question requires. | attribution: The answer correctly surfaces the conflicting historical traditions regarding Romulus's death and accurately attributes the distinct accounts to Livy and Plutarch in clear prose.

results.question: How did Themistocles die?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides states plainly that disease was the real cause of his death, noting only as a rumor the story that he poisoned himself on finding he could not deliver Greece to the Persian king. Plutarch gives the dramatic suicide as "the usual story" — that Themistocles sacrificed, feasted with his friends, and drank bull's blood — while acknowledging the alternative that it was a fast-acting poison.

results.question: Did Caligula actually intend to make his horse consul?
results.ideal_answer: The two sources differ in confidence. Suetonius reports it only as hearsay — "it is even said that he intended to make him consul." Cassius Dio states it more strongly: that Caligula promised the consulship to the horse Incitatus and "would certainly have done" it had he lived longer. So the claim is a guarded rumor in Suetonius and a near-assertion in Dio.
results.answer:
The ancient sources differ on this. Suetonius presents the story as a rumour, stating that Caligula lavished extraordinary luxuries on his horse Incitatus — a marble stable, an ivory manger, purple housings, a jewelled frontlet, and a house with slaves — and adds cautiously: "It is even said that he intended to make him consul" [1]. The phrase "it is even said" (Latin *dicitur*) signals that Suetonius is reporting hearsay rather than confirmed fact.

Cassius Dio is more emphatic. He writes that Caligula "went to such an excess of folly as to promise to appoint his horse consul" [c1077], and elsewhere that "he wished to appoint his horse consul" [c1437]. Dio thus treats the intention as genuine and directly attributable to Caligula, framing it as part of his larger pattern of madness and self-deification.

In short, both sources agree Caligula at least talked about making Incitatus consul, but they differ in certainty: Suetonius treats it as a reported claim, while Cassius Dio presents it as a deliberate promise and real wish. Caligula was assassinated in 41 CE before any such appointment could occur.

results.question: How did Bucephalus die?
results.ideal_answer: The sources disagree on whether the horse died of battle wounds or of old age. Arrian insists explicitly that Bucephalas died "not from having been wounded by any one, but from the effects of toil and old age." Plutarch reports both: that most authorities say he died under treatment for his wounds after the battle with Porus, though Onesicritus says it was fatigue and age. Both agree he was about thirty and that Alexander founded a city (Bucephala) in his memory.
results.answer: According to Plutarch, Bucephalus died 'as most of the authorities state, under cure of his wounds, or as Onesicritus says, of fatigue and age, being thirty years old' [1]. Arrian, in his Anabasis of Alexander, explicitly says that Bucephalus died 'not from having been wounded by any one, but from the effects of toil and old age; for he was about thirty years old, and quite worn out with toil' [2]. Thus, the sources disagree: the majority account (as reported by Plutarch) attributes his death to wounds received in the battle with Porus, while Onesicritus and Arrian attribute it to fatigue and old age.

results.question: How did Xerxes get back to Asia after Salamis?
results.ideal_answer: Herodotus himself records two conflicting accounts. In his main narrative, Xerxes marched his army by land to the Hellespont and crossed to Abydos by ship, the bridges having been broken by a storm. But he then reports "another story": that Xerxes sailed from Eion on the Strymon in a Phoenician ship caught in a storm, and that the Persian nobles on deck leapt into the sea to lighten it and save the king — a tale Herodotus relates but disbelieves.
results.answer:
After the defeat at Salamis, Xerxes retreated overland to the Hellespont with a portion of his army, following the same route he had taken during his advance into Greece [1]. He was accompanied by a force of 60,000 men under Artabazus [1][5]. The march was arduous: his troops suffered severely from famine and sickness along the way [3].

When Xerxes reached the Hellespont, he found that his bridge of boats had been destroyed by a storm [2][1][3]. Consequently, he could not cross as he had planned. Instead, he crossed the strait into Asia on board a vessel (or vessels) [2][1][3]. According to some accounts, this crossing was perilous and his ship narrowly escaped shipwreck during the passage [3].

Regarding his state of mind, the sources differ. Some ancient accounts, particularly Greek ones, portrayed Xerxes as fleeing in panic and despair after the defeat [1][5]. However, modern historian J. B. Bury argues that this was a dramatic exaggeration by the Greeks, and that Xerxes' retreat was a calculated strategic decision: he needed to secure his line of retreat to Asia, especially given the risk of a revolt among the Ionian Greeks, while leaving Mardonius behind with a large army to continue the campaign the following spring [5].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The claim that Xerxes was in despair is grounded in source [3], but the answer misattributes it to ancient Greek accounts (citing [1] and [5]), which is a wrong-source error; all other claims are well-supported. | completeness: The answer fully describes Xerxes' overland march to the Hellespont and his subsequent crossing into Asia by ship after the bridges were destroyed, directly answering the question, and it is not penalized for omitting the full alternative Eion account or extra historiographical details that go beyond what was asked. | attribution: The answer correctly surfaces the disagreement regarding Xerxes' state of mind and reasons for retreating, explicitly attributing the contrasting views to ancient accounts and modern historian J. results.question: How did Remus die?
results.ideal_answer: Livy gives two versions side by side: that Remus fell in the brawl that broke out when the brothers' rival bird-omens were disputed, or — the "more common account" — that he leaped over Romulus's newly-built walls in derision and Romulus killed him in a rage, crying "So shall every one fare, who shall dare to leap over my fortifications." Smith's history follows the leaping-the-wall version as the standard one.
results.answer:
According to the sources in the corpus, Remus died during a dispute with his twin brother Romulus over the founding of Rome, but the details vary by author:

**Livy** presents two versions. In the first, after a contested augury (Remus saw six vultures, Romulus twelve), the brothers and their followers met in heated altercation that turned to bloodshed, and "Remus fell from a blow received in the crowd" [3]. The "more common account," Livy says, is that Remus, in derision of his brother, leaped over Romulus's newly built wall, and Romulus slew him in a passion, declaring: "So shall every one fare, who shall dare to leap over my fortifications" [3].

**Plutarch** records that after an augury dispute in which Remus accused Romulus of fraud, Remus ridiculed and obstructed Romulus's work on the city ditch. Then, "as he was in contempt leaping over it, some say Romulus himself struck him, others Celer, one of his companions" [12]. Remus fell, and in the ensuing scuffle his foster-father Faustulus and Faustulus's brother Plistinus were also killed [12]. Romulus afterward buried Remus and his foster-fathers on the mount Remonia [11].

**Cassius Dio** (preserved via Zonaras) states more simply that a dispute arose over sovereignty and the city, and "they got into a conflict in which Remus was killed" [1], adding that from this arose the custom that anyone who crossed the camp trench by unusual paths should be put to death [1].

**William Smith**, in his summary history, follows the leaping-over-the-wall version: Remus leapt over the Pomoerium wall in scorn, and Romulus slew him, exclaiming "So die whosoever hereafter shall leap over my walls" [6].

In short, all sources agree Remus died at the hands of Romulus (or his party) in a quarrel over the founding of the city. The most widely circulated account has him leaping over the new wall in mockery and being struck down for it; Livy and Plutarch both note this as the more common or one variant, while also recording alternative details.

results.question: How did Alexander deal with the Gordian knot?
results.ideal_answer: The sources split on whether he cut it or untied it by a trick. Arrian reports that some say Alexander, unable to loosen the cord, struck it with his sword and cut it through — but that Aristobulus says he pulled out the pin of the wagon-pole and so drew out the yoke. Plutarch gives the same two versions: most authors say he cut it with his sword, but Aristobulus says he undid it easily by pulling out the pin.
results.answer: Alexander dealt with the Gordian knot by cutting it with his sword when he could not untie it [1][4][2][3]. According to Plutarch, most authors tell the story that finding himself unable to untie the knot, whose ends were secretly twisted round and folded up within it, he cut it asunder with his sword [3]. Arrian also reports that some say he struck the cord with his sword and cut it through, declaring it had been untied [2]. Both Arrian and Plutarch additionally record a variant account from Aristobulus: that Alexander pulled out the pin of the wagon-pole, which held the cord together, and drew out the yoke, thus untying it without cutting [2][3]. Arrian himself states he cannot affirm with confidence how the feat was performed [2]. In either version, the act was accepted as fulfilling the oracle that whoever loosened the knot would rule Asia, and the gods ratified it with thunder and lightning that night [2][1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided passages, with accurate attributions and no invented content. | completeness: The answer fully addresses how Alexander dealt with the knot by detailing both the sword-cutting and pin-pulling accounts, the disagreement between sources, and Arrian’s uncertainty, leaving no part of the question unanswered. | attribution: The answer successfully surfaces the disagreement among the sources regarding how Alexander solved the Gordian knot and clearly attributes the differing accounts (cutting the cord versus removing the pin) to the specific authors in prose.

results.question: What did Nero say about an artist dying, and when?
results.ideal_answer: The wording and timing differ between sources. Suetonius reports that as his grave was dug before his eyes Nero wept and kept repeating "What an artist is now about to perish!" Cassius Dio gives a different form and moment: that Nero spoke the "far-famed sentence" — "Jupiter, what an artist perishes in me!" — at the very instant he killed himself.

results.question: Why did Tarpeia betray the citadel, and how did she die?
results.ideal_answer: All sources agree she was bribed by the gold the Sabines wore on their left arms and was crushed to death under their shields, but they differ on her intent. Livy gives the standard treacherous version (she was bribed to admit the soldiers and crushed under their arms) but also reports an alternative: that she demanded their shields in good faith, meaning to disarm them, and was killed as if a traitor by the very reward she chose. Plutarch agrees she coveted the golden bracelets and asked for "the things they wore on their left arms."
results.answer:
According to the ancient sources, Tarpeia betrayed the Roman citadel out of greed for gold. The most common tradition, found in both Plutarch and Livy, states that she was the daughter of Spurius Tarpeius, the commander of the Capitol, and was dazzled by the golden bracelets and rings worn by the Sabine soldiers on their left arms [2][4][3]. She struck a bargain with the Sabine king Titus Tatius to open the gates in exchange for "what they wore on their left arms" — meaning the gold jewelry [2][3]. Livy adds the detail that she had gone outside the walls to fetch water for a sacrifice when she admitted the armed Sabines [4].

As for her death, once the Sabines had entered the citadel, they fulfilled her request literally but treacherously: instead of giving her only their golden bracelets, they also threw their heavy shields — which were likewise carried on the left arm — upon her. The weight of the gold and shields crushed her to death [1][4][3]. Plutarch records that Tatius himself was the first to cast his bracelet and buckler at her, and the rest of the Sabines followed suit, burying her under the heap [1]. Livy offers two interpretations of the Sabines' motive: either they wanted it to appear that the citadel had been taken by storm, or they wished to set a precedent that no faith should ever be kept with a traitor [4]. He also notes a variant in which Tarpeia, understanding the ambiguity of her request, actually demanded their shields expressly — and was thus killed by the reward of her own choosing [4].

Plutarch discusses several alternative traditions and dismisses them as absurd: one claimed that Tarpeia was actually the daughter of Tatius himself and acted under her father's contrivance while forcibly detained by Romulus; another, by the poet Simylus, preposterously held that she betrayed the Capitol not to the Sabines but to the Gauls, having fallen in love with their king, and was killed in the same manner by their shields [1]. After her death, Tarpeia was buried on the hill, which thereafter bore her name until King Tarquin rededicated it to Jupiter. Only the Tarpeian Rock — the cliff from which traitors were later hurled — preserved her name [1][3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer—from Tarpeia's greed for gold and her bargain over what the Sabines wore on their left arms, to her death under shields and bracelets, Livy's variant motives, and the later history of the Tarpeian Rock—is directly supported by the provided passages. | completeness: The answer fully addresses both parts of the question—her greed for gold as the motive and being crushed under the Sabines' shields—while also providing additional context without omitting anything asked. | attribution: The sources agree on the core narrative while providing complementary details and reported variants, and the answer accurately attributes these specific contributions to their respective authors in prose.

results.question: What was Lycurgus' date and life story?
results.ideal_answer: The sources cannot agree on almost anything about him. Plutarch opens his Life by warning that scarcely anything asserted by one historian is not contradicted by another — as to his family, his travels, the manner of his death, and above all the very age in which he lived. Grote sharpens the specific disagreement: Herodotus makes Lycurgus uncle and guardian of king Labotas of the Agid line (placing him about 996 BC), whereas all other accounts make him a younger brother of the rival Proclid line.
results.answer: Plutarch’s *Life of Lycurgus* stresses that there is no agreement among ancient authorities about when Lycurgus lived [11]. Some (including Aristotle) placed him in the time of Iphitus, with whom he was said to have established the Olympic truce, pointing to an inscription on a quoit. Other chronologers, like Eratosthenes and Apollodorus, argued from the Spartan king‑lists that he was much older than the Olympic games. Timaeus conjectured that there were two men named Lycurgus, the elder of whom lived not long after Homer (and some even said he had seen Homer) [11]. The sources also disagree on his parentage: Simonides made him son of Prytanis, while most others gave his father as Eunomus, making him the eleventh in descent from Heracles [2].\n\nHis life‑story is equally varied. He became regent for his infant nephew Charilaus, but to avoid suspicion of plotting against the child he went into voluntary exile [5]. He visited Crete, studied its laws, and brought back the poet Thales to prepare Sparta for his reforms [8]. He also travelled to Asia, where he collected the poems of Homer, and some say he even went to Egypt [8][3]. Returning to a Sparta in turmoil, he consulted the Delphic oracle, which called him “beloved of God, and rather God than man” and promised that his laws would be the best [3]. He then carried out a thorough reformation of the Spartan state. During the political struggle he was attacked by the young Alcander, who struck out one of his eyes, but Lycurgus forgave him and turned him into an admirer [7]. After his laws were established he made the Spartans swear to keep them unchanged until his return, then went to Delphi and, having received the oracle’s approval, starved himself to death so that his countrymen could never be released from their oath [4]. Conflicting reports place his death in Cirrha, Elis, or Crete; some say his body was burned and the ashes scattered in the sea [1].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided passages, with no invented or unsupported content. | completeness: The answer thoroughly covers both parts of the question by explaining the conflicting ancient accounts regarding Lycurgus' date and providing a detailed, complete narrative of his life story. | attribution: All provided passages are from the same source (Plutarch) and do not disagree with one another; the model accurately attributes the overall narrative to Plutarch and correctly names the historical sub-sources he mentions without any misattribution.

results.question: Who was to blame for losing the battle of Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: Both sources fault the consul Varro (Gaius Terentius), but with different emphasis. Polybius lays the disaster squarely on him personally — his flight to Venusia was "as disgraceful to himself as his conduct in office had been disastrous to his country." Mommsen, while equally scornful of "the hero of the pavement," frames the deeper fault as systemic: the old custom of alternating supreme command between the two consuls day by day, which forced the cautious Paullus to yield to Varro's will.

results.question: How was the city of Nineveh captured and the Assyrian empire brought to an end?
results.ideal_answer: The accounts disagree on how Nineveh fell. In the version Herodotus and Rawlinson follow, it was taken by a Median assault: Cyaxares, allied with the Babylonian Nabopolassar, pressed the siege until the Assyrian king's means of resistance were exhausted and he burned himself in his palace. A rival tradition, reported from Ctesias, has the walls breached not by storm but by nature — an unusually wet season swelled the Tigris until it burst in and swept away more than two miles of wall, after which the king set fire to himself. Both traditions end with the king's self-immolation, but differ on whether the city fell to siege or to flood.

results.question: How large was the army that Xerxes led against Greece?
results.ideal_answer: Herodotus gives an enormous figure: the land army alone amounted to one hundred and seventy myriads (1,700,000), a total he says was reached by herding the troops in ten-thousand-man batches into a walled enclosure and counting how many times it filled. Rawlinson rejects the number as untestable and almost certainly grossly exaggerated — armies, he notes, are never really counted, and so crude a method invited inflation — though he allows the host probably did exceed a million.
results.answer: According to Herodotus, the primary ancient source, Xerxes' army was enormous. The land force alone, counted at Doriscus by packing men into an enclosure built around 10,000 soldiers, was said to comprise 1,700,000 infantry and 80,000 cavalry, with an additional 20,000 men in chariots or on camels, for a total of 1,800,000 land troops [1][3][5]. The fleet consisted of 1,207 triremes and 3,000 smaller vessels; counting 200 crew per trireme and 80 per smaller ship, the naval personnel added roughly 517,000 men, bringing the total force from Asia to about 2,317,000 [4][5]. As Xerxes marched through Thrace and Macedonia, he pressed additional troops and ships into service, adding approximately 300,000 land troops and 24,000 naval crew, raising the total fighting force to 2,641,000 [4]. Herodotus further estimated that camp-followers, attendants, and crews of supply vessels were at least as numerous as the fighting men, doubling the figure to approximately 5,283,220 men — not counting women, eunuchs, or animals [2][4]. Modern historians such as George Grote and George Rawlinson report these figures but express strong skepticism, noting that the counting method was crude and prone to exaggeration, and that the true number, while still vast, was likely far smaller [1][3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim—including troop counts, fleet numbers, the doubling of personnel with camp-followers, and modern historians' skepticism about Herodotus's totals—is supported by the provided passages, even where figures are rounded or derived by subtraction from source totals. | completeness: The answer fully addresses the question by providing Herodotus's figures for the land and naval forces, describing the counting method, and noting modern historians' skepticism, covering everything asked without omitting any required information. | attribution: The sources agree on the historical figures reported by Herodotus and the modern skepticism regarding them, and the answer correctly attributes these points without any misattribution.

results.question: How did Hannibal die when he was cornered at the court of King Prusias?
results.ideal_answer: The common account is that Hannibal, finding the house surrounded by Prusias's soldiers (sent at the demand of the Roman envoy Flamininus), took poison he had long kept ready and drank it; Livy and Appian both give this poison version. Plutarch, however, records rival traditions about the manner of his death: some said he strangled himself by twisting his cloak about his neck with a servant's help, and others that he drank bull's blood.

results.question: How large was Hannibal's army when he descended into Italy after crossing the Alps?
results.ideal_answer: The sources do not agree. Polybius gives a precise, low figure — twelve thousand Libyan and eight thousand Iberian infantry and not more than six thousand cavalry — and says he took it from a bronze tablet Hannibal himself set up at the Lacinian promontory. Livy reports that the number is by no means agreed upon by authors: the highest estimates run to a hundred thousand foot and twenty thousand horse, the lowest to twenty thousand foot and six thousand horse (this last agreeing with Polybius).
results.answer: When Hannibal descended into Italy after crossing the Alps, his army numbered about 20,000 infantry and 6,000 cavalry. This figure comes from Hannibal's own statement as recorded by Polybius (implied) and explicitly given by later historians [5][6]. Polybius notes that at the Rhone crossing Hannibal had 38,000 infantry and more than 8,000 cavalry, and that he lost nearly half in the passage of the Alps [3]. Livy reports that the numbers are disputed: the highest estimates give 100,000 foot and 20,000 horse, the lowest 20,000 foot and 6,000 horse, and he cites L. Cincius Alimentus who heard from Hannibal that he lost 36,000 men after the Rhone [2]. The most commonly cited figure from Hannibal himself is therefore 20,000 infantry and 6,000 cavalry.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer accurately reports the 20,000 infantry and 6,000 cavalry figure and the disputed ancient estimates, but it misattributes that final total to Polybius (implied), whereas Polybius only gives the Rhone numbers and says 'nearly half' were lost; the exact post-Alps total is explicitly given by Mommsen and Smith. | completeness: The answer states the army's size (20,000 infantry and 6,000 cavalry), notes that sources disagree, and provides Livy's range of estimates, fully covering what the question asks without omitting any required information. | attribution: The answer effectively surfaces the historical disagreement regarding the size of Hannibal's army and clearly attributes the distinct estimates and contributions to Polybius, Livy, and later historians in prose.

results.question: How many Romans were killed at the battle of Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: Polybius puts the Roman dead at about seventy thousand — nearly the whole army, since of the infantry actually engaged only some three thousand escaped. Livy gives a smaller toll: forty thousand foot and two thousand seven hundred horse slain, with an equal number of allies among them.
results.answer: According to Livy, 40,000 Roman infantry and 2,700 cavalry were slain at Cannae [1]. Polybius reports that 70,000 of those actually engaged died, with an additional 10,000 taken prisoner [2]. Appian gives a figure of 50,000 killed, along with many prisoners [3]. Mommsen, writing as a modern historian but using ancient sources, states that of the 76,000 Romans in the line, 70,000 were slain [4]. Thus, the ancient sources disagree on the precise number, with estimates ranging from about 42,700 to 70,000 dead.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims—including the casualty figures from Livy, Polybius, Appian, and Mommsen and the concluding range—are directly supported by the provided passages, with no invented content. | completeness: The answer provides the casualty figures from Polybius and Livy (along with extra sources) directly addressing how many Romans were killed, omitting no required information. | attribution: The answer correctly identifies the disagreement among the sources regarding the number of Roman casualties and explicitly attributes each distinct figure to its respective author in prose.

results.question: What was the real cause of the Second Punic War?
results.ideal_answer: The sources frame the war's origin differently. Polybius insists that the siege of Saguntum and the crossing of the Ebro were only the first acts of the war, not its causes, and faults the historians who stop there; the true causes, he argues, lay deeper. Livy grounds the war in Saguntum and in Hannibal's inherited enmity — the boy's altar-oath to be an enemy of Rome, and Hamilcar's lasting grievance over the loss of Sicily and Sardinia.
results.answer: According to Polybius, the real causes of the Second Punic War were threefold. First, the resentment of Hamilcar Barca over the outcome of the First Punic War and his personal desire for revenge against Rome [3]. Second, the Roman seizure of Sardinia and the imposition of an additional heavy indemnity on Carthage after the Mercenary War — an act which Polybius calls “the second and the most important cause” [3]. Third, the growth of Carthaginian power in Iberia, which gave them the confidence to embark on war [3]. The attack on Saguntum was merely a pretext adopted by Hannibal in a fit of passion, when he should have demanded the return of Sardinia and the remission of the tribute as the true grounds for war [1]. Polybius concludes that if Saguntum is taken as the cause, the Carthaginians were in the wrong; but if the seizure of Sardinia and the imposed tribute are considered the causes, the Carthaginians had good reason for war [4].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: All claims are fully grounded in the provided passages, with no invented content. | completeness: The answer fully addresses the question by explaining Polybius's analysis of the true underlying causes (Hamilcar's grievance, the seizure of Sardinia and indemnity, and Carthaginian expansion in Iberia) and correctly identifies Saguntum as a pretext, which completely answers what was asked without needing the additional historiographical context found in the reference. | attribution: The provided passages all come from the same author (Polybius) and present a unified argument regarding the causes of the war, so the answer correctly attributes the claims to him without needing to surface any disagreements.

results.question: Who won the battle of Mantinea, and what did it settle?
results.ideal_answer: The verdict is disputed. Xenophon ends his history on a deliberately bleak note: both sides set up trophies and claimed victory, yet neither gained any territory or advantage, and uncertainty and confusion in Greece were tenfold greater after the battle than before — it settled nothing. Diodorus, by contrast, counts it a victory for the Boeotians (Thebans), the verdict the dying Epaminondas is given before he allows the spear to be drawn from his body.
results.answer:
The question can refer to two different battles of Mantinea, the most important ones being:

1. **The battle of Mantinea in 418 BC** (during the Peloponnesian War): the **Lacedaemonians (Spartans)** won. Thucydides reports that “the Lacedaemonians had the advantage in the battle, and after erecting a trophy, returned home, having set free the Mantineans, and given back Tegea to the Tegeans” [c1256]. He adds that “by this battle the Lacedaemonians had retrieved their reputation, which had suffered from the misfortune at Pylos, and from the affair at Cythera and other reverses” [c1254]. The settlement was the restoration of Spartan military prestige and dominance in the Peloponnese; as Grote says, it “wiped off at once the preexisting stain upon the honour of Sparta … and replaced Sparta in her old position of military preeminence before the eyes of Greece” [1].

2. **The battle of Mantinea in 362 BC** (the Theban–Spartan conflict): the **Thebans** under Epaminondas won a tactical victory, but both sides claimed success. Xenophon ends his *Hellenica* by describing the battle as “the greatest that had ever taken place between Greeks to that day … Yet, great as it was, it decided nothing. For when the battle was over, the confusion and uncertainty in Greece was greater than before” [c2182]. Epaminondas was mortally wounded, and his dying injunction was to make peace. Accordingly, a peace was concluded that recognized the *status quo*, leaving Megalopolis and the independent Messênê in place, while Sparta alone protested [6]. The settlement, therefore, was a general peace that preserved the existing political situation (including an independent Messene) and left the Greek world without any dominant hegemon [6][c2182].

results.question: What happened to the captured Athenian generals Nicias and Demosthenes after the Sicilian disaster?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides says the Syracusans put Nicias and Demosthenes to death — butchered against the wishes of the Spartan Gylippus, who had hoped to carry the captured enemy generals home to Sparta. Plutarch preserves a rival account, which he credits to Timaeus: that they were not executed by the Syracusans at all, but, warned by a message from Hermocrates and with the connivance of their guards, were allowed to put an end to themselves.
results.answer: After the Sicilian disaster, the Athenian generals Nicias and Demosthenes were captured by the Syracusans. Demosthenes attempted suicide but was prevented and taken prisoner [5]. Both were subsequently put to death, against the will of the Spartan Gylippus, who had wanted to take them back to Sparta as prisoners [2][1]. Gylippus considered Demosthenes an enemy due to his success at Pylos, while Nicias was seen as a friend of Sparta for his efforts to release Spartan prisoners [2][1]. However, the Syracusans and their allies, particularly the Corinthians, insisted on their execution. Some Syracusans who had secretly corresponded with Nicias feared he might reveal their names under torture, while the Corinthians worried that Nicias's wealth would allow him to escape and cause further harm [2][1]. According to Grote, Hermocrates privately arranged for the generals to die by their own hands before the execution [1]. Thucydides notes that Nicias, of all the Greeks of his time, least deserved such a fate, given his virtuous life [2].

results.question: How do the sources judge Alcibiades?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides judges that his lawless personal extravagance — horses and expenditure beyond his means — made the people fear him as an aspiring tyrant, "and this later on had not a little to do with the ruin of the Athenian state," though his public war conduct was excellent. Plutarch fixes on his chameleon-like versatility, adapting to Spartan austerity or Persian pomp at will. Grote, the modern voice, grants him boldness, resource, and vigor "not surpassed by any contemporary Greek" while damning his want of public principle. Bury, a second modern voice, judges him a born politician but no statesman — lacking the purpose, perseverance, and self-control that statesmanship demands.

results.question: How do the sources judge Themistocles?

Thucydides' obituary calls his untaught native genius for instant judgment and prediction "quite extraordinary and unparalleled." Grote balances the picture: his rapacity made him hated and feared among the allies, and the suspicions of treasonable medism at Athens grew from his own corrupt and double-faced conduct. Bury, a later modern voice, credits the man of genius who made Athens a sea-power as the greatest of all her statesmen.

results.question: How is the disaster at Cannae described?
results.ideal_answer: Polybius gives the soldier's accounting: of six thousand Roman horse only seventy escaped with Varro, the infantry "died nobly" to the number of seventy thousand, both sides fighting with conspicuous gallantry. Dio's fragment calls it the most fearful disaster Rome ever suffered, preceded by portents and Sibylline prophecy. Mommsen, the modern judge, treats the "unexampled success" as the moment Hannibal's grand political combination against Rome seemed to mature. Appian tallies the toll — 50,000 Roman dead in a few hours, with senators, every tribune and centurion, and both their best generals among them. Livy registers the reaction at home: never, while the city stood safe, was there so great a panic, at the loss of two consular armies and their consuls.
results.answer: The disaster at Cannae is described across multiple sources, each emphasizing different facets of the catastrophe. Livy reports the battle as comparable to the defeat at the Allia but far bloodier, noting that 'forty thousand foot, two thousand seven hundred horse ... are said to have been slain' [1]. He adds that scarcely seventy men accompanied the fleeing consul Varro, and that the greater part of the army perished with the other consul [1]. Plutarch highlights the tactical envelopment by Hannibal's forces, causing the Romans to be cut down on their flanks and rear, and recounts the death of consul Aemilius Paulus, who refused to flee and sat down on a stone awaiting death [2]. Polybius focuses on the aftermath, writing that the Romans 'despaired of retaining their supremacy over the Italians, and were in the greatest alarm' after the defeat [3]. Mommsen provides a detailed narrative of the battle's mechanics—the Roman infantry being caught in a pincer movement and annihilated—and states that out of 76,000 Romans, 70,000 fell on the field, including the consul Paullus and eighty senators [4]. Appian similarly records that '50,000 of their soldiers were slain and a great many taken prisoners' and that the battle 'is still famous among the Romans as a disaster' [5]. Thus, the accounts uniformly portray Cannae as an unprecedented slaughter marked by immense loss of life, the death of chief commanders, and a profound crisis for the Roman state.

results.question: How do historians ancient and modern assess Pericles?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides' famous verdict: by rank, ability, and known integrity he led the multitude instead of being led, so that "what was nominally a democracy became in his hands government by the first citizen." Plutarch gives the deathbed self-assessment — his proudest claim was that "no Athenian, through my means, ever wore mourning." Grote notes that his immense ascendancy and unparalleled eloquence are attested even more forcibly by his enemies than his friends. Bury, more measured, rates him a man of finer fibre than Themistocles yet no statesman of originative genius — one who perfected the ideas of others rather than creating them.

results.question: What did ancient writers know and say about Britain and its people?
results.ideal_answer: Caesar, the first eyewitness, reports inland Britons who sow no corn, live on milk and flesh, dress in skins, dye themselves blue with woad, and hold wives in common. Tacitus weighs their origins from physical type — the ruddy-haired Caledonians suggesting German stock, the swarthy Silures Iberian. Strabo maps the island as a triangle parallel to the Gallic coast, roughly 4,300–4,400 stadia along its longest side. Cassius Dio, writing later, divides the Britons into two great races, the Caledonians and the Maeatians, who dwell coatless and shoeless in tents, hold their women in common, and rear their children as a community.
results.answer:
Ancient writers knew Britain as the largest island known to the Romans [7] and recorded a wide range of observations about its geography, climate, natural resources, inhabitants, and customs.

**Geography and shape.** Tacitus, drawing on Livy and Fabius Rusticus, compares Britain's outline to an oblong target or a two-edged axe, though he notes that Caledonia stretches out irregularly like a wedge [7]. Caesar describes the island as triangular, estimating its circumference at about 2,000 miles [2]. The Roman fleet under Agricola first circumnavigated Britain, proving it was an island, and discovered the Orcades (Orkneys) [7].

**Climate and daylight.** Caesar found the climate more temperate than Gaul, with less severe cold [2]. Tacitus notes frequent rain and cloud but no extreme cold; he reports that at the island's northern extremity summer nights are so short that the sun seems merely to move across the sky [1]. Strabo similarly remarks on the heavy mist and rain, saying that even on clear days the sun appears only for three or four hours about noon [3].

**Natural resources and exports.** Tacitus notes gold, silver, and other metals, plus pearls (though he judges them cloudy and livid) [1]. Caesar confirms tin in the midlands and iron on the coast, but says iron is scarce and brass is imported; timber is abundant except beech and fir [2]. Strabo provides a fuller list of exports: corn, cattle, gold, silver, iron, skins, slaves, and hunting-dogs prized even for war [3]. He adds that the Britons pay moderate import and export duties on goods from Gaul—ivory bracelets, amber, glass vessels, and trinkets [30]. 

**Inhabitants and origins.** Tacitus says the origin of the first inhabitants is obscure. He associates the ruddy-haired, large-limbed Caledonians with a German derivation; the swarthy, curly-haired Silures opposite Spain with an Iberian origin; and those nearest Gaul with Gauls [9]. Caesar says the interior people are indigenous, while the coastal tribes come from Belgic Gaul [2]. The languages and sacred rites of the Britons and Gauls, according to Tacitus, differ little [9].

**Physical appearance and dress.** The Britons, in Caesar's account, dye themselves with woad, producing a bluish colour to look more terrible in battle; they wear their hair long and shave every part of the body except the head and upper lip [6]. Strabo, who saw British youths at Rome, describes them as taller than the Celts, with lighter hair, but notes bow-legs and a lack of symmetry [3]. Cassius Dio says the northern Britons—Caledonians and Maeatians—live coatless and shoeless in tents [10].

**Customs and society.** Caesar reports that the inland Britons do not sow corn but live on milk and flesh and wear skins, while the more civilised inhabitants of Kent differ little from the Gauls [6][2]. He records that ten or even twelve men, especially brothers and fathers with sons, share wives in common, though any children are assigned to the first husband [6]. Cassius Dio notes a similar practice among the Maeatians and Caledonians, who rear all offspring communally [10]. Strabo says some Britons lack the skill to make cheese despite abundant milk, and are ignorant of gardening and husbandry; their “cities” are enclosures of felled trees in forests, where they make huts and pen their cattle [3].

**Druids and religion.** Caesar states that the druidic institution is believed to have originated in Britain, and that those who wish to study it most thoroughly travel there [13]. He describes the Druids' doctrines—including the transmigration of souls to encourage valour—and their sacrifices, which involve burning men alive in colossal wicker images [12]. Tacitus confirms that the sacred rites and superstitions of the Britons are discernibly the same as those of the Gauls [9].

**Warfare.** Tacitus and Caesar both emphasise the use of chariots. Caesar provides the classic description: charioteers drive about throwing javelins, then leap down to fight on foot while the drivers wait to retrieve them [27][9]. Tacitus adds that the most honourable man guides the reins while his dependents fight from the chariot [9]. Cassius Dio describes the Caledonians' and Maeatians' weapons—shield, short spear with a rattling bronze apple, and daggers—and their ability to endure hunger and cold, even hiding in swamps with only their heads above water for days [10]. He also says they live by pasturage, hunting, and a few fruit-trees, and do not eat the plentiful fish [10].

**Political organisation.** Tacitus reports that the Britons were formerly governed by kings but are now divided into factions under chiefs, a lack of union that works to Rome's advantage [9]. Notable royal figures include Boadicea, a woman of royal descent who led a massive revolt, sacking Roman cities and slaughtering thousands before being defeated [16][17][20]; Caractacus, a king betrayed by Queen Cartismandua of the Brigantes [19]; and Venutius, Cartismandua's spurned husband who subsequently waged war on the Romans [19].

**Later Roman experience.** Ammianus Marcellinus records that in the mid-4th century AD Britain was ravaged by the Picts (divided into Dicalidones and Vecturiones), the Attacotti, and the Scots, while Franks and Saxons raided the Gallic coast [21]. The general Theodosius (father of the future emperor) was despatched to restore order, landing at Richborough and marching on London [23].

**Caesar’s assessment.** Plutarch says that Caesar’s British expedition was famous for carrying Roman arms into the western ocean, but he adds that the islanders were so miserably poor that there was nothing worth plundering [28]. Caesar himself had reported that Britain contained tin, iron, timber, and cattle, but that its people were numerous, had many buildings, and used iron or bronze rings for money [2].

results.question: How is Cleopatra's death reported across the sources?
results.ideal_answer: All three sources report the famous asp while admitting uncertainty. Plutarch describes her found dead in royal ornaments on a golden bed, Iras dying at her feet and Charmion adjusting her diadem — but says "what really took place is known to no one," weighing the asp in the figs against poison in a hollow bodkin. Dio likewise: "no one knows clearly in what manner she perished," only slight indentations on her arm. Suetonius adds that Augustus, wanting her alive for his triumph, sent the Psylli snake-charmers to suck out the poison.
results.answer: The ancient sources report Cleopatra’s death in divergent ways. Plutarch says no one knows what really happened: some say an asp was hidden among figs and bit her bare arm after she saw it; others say she kept the asp in a vase and pricked it with a golden spindle; still others claim she carried poison in a hollow hair‑bodkin. Only two faint puncture‑marks were found on her arm, and Octavian’s triumph included a figure of Cleopatra with an asp clinging to her [3][2]. Cassius Dio likewise states that the manner of her death is unclear; slight indentations were found on her arm; some report an asp brought in a water‑jar or among flowers, while others allege she smeared a needle (worn in her hair) with a poison that caused quick, painless death when a tiny scratch allowed it to touch blood [1]. Suetonius writes that Octavian “anxiously wished to save” Cleopatra for his triumph “and when she was supposed to have been bit to death by an asp, he sent for the Psylli to endeavour to suck out the poison” [6]. Appian records two versions: she “took an asp and applied it to her arm, and so died without pain; others say she died by the bite of an asp which was brought to her in a basket of figs”; in any case she was found dead with her two women, and Octavian carried an image of her with the asp on her arm in his triumph [c16338]. Livy’s epitome says that Cleopatra first experimented on slaves to find the easiest death and then “applied an asp to her breast, and died” [c19969]. In stark contrast, Josephus in both the Antiquities and the Jewish War reports that she was taken captive by Augustus, exhibited in his triumph, and afterwards sent back to Egypt, where “she lived in great splendor, and was in no small reputation” — no suicide is mentioned [c11987][c15380]. Thus, while the majority of extant Greco‑Roman accounts present her as dying by an asp bite (or another self‑administered poison), Josephus offers a wholly different tradition in which she survived captivity and lived on in prosperity.

results.question: What do the sources say about Alexander's final illness and death?
results.ideal_answer: Both Plutarch and Arrian follow the Royal Diary: a fever after drinking with Medius, worsening day by day as he continued bathing and sacrificing, until he lay speechless — Arrian preserving the scene of the soldiers filing past while he could only greet them with his eyes — and died. Both record the later poisoning story; Plutarch calls it "a mere made-up story," noting the body stayed uncorrupted for days, and Arrian notes Aristobulus and Ptolemy agree with the diary, adding only the alleged last word that the kingdom would go "to the best." Diodorus, the other surviving ancient narrative, closes the twelve-year reign and reports the same poisoning rumour (Antipater's son Iollas as cup-bearer). Grote, the modern judge, weighs that rumour and concludes it was fomented by the enemies of Antipater and Cassander, above all the rancorous Olympias.

results.question: How do Roman writers describe the Germans' way of fighting?
results.ideal_answer: Caesar, from battlefield experience, describes them rapidly forming a phalanx so dense his soldiers leapt onto it to tear away shields from above. Tacitus, the ethnographer, details the framea — the short, sharp, all-purpose spear used at close quarters or thrown — the wedge-shaped battle line, the doctrine that giving ground to rally again is prudence rather than cowardice, and the carrying off of their dead mid-battle. Tacitus' Annals adds, through Germanicus' battle-speech, that the German goes without cuirass or helmet and carries only a flimsy shield of osiers or painted board. Ammianus, centuries later, shows the Alamanni in action: charging with javelins, hair streaming and eyes blazing, against the locked Roman shield-wall.

results.question: How was Caesar's funeral described, and what did it unleash?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius describes the planned pyre in the Campus Martius overtaken by the crowd: two armed men fired the bier before the Rostra and the throng heaped on benches and court furniture; afterwards the populace ran with torches to the houses of Brutus and Cassius and lynched the wrong Cinna. Plutarch adds the cause: Antony's funeral oration, climaxing in the unfolding of Caesar's bloody, pierced garment, after which the mob burned the body on an improvised pyre and ran with the half-burnt brands to fire the murderers' houses. Cassius Dio reports the same inflamed mob and the lynching of the innocent Helvius Cinna in mistake for the praetor of that name. Appian adds the decisive stagecraft: a wax effigy of Caesar revolved on a mechanical device to display all twenty-three wounds, which tipped the crowd into burning the body and the senate-house.
results.answer:
The funeral of Julius Caesar is described in vivid and mutually corroborating detail by Plutarch, Suetonius, Appian, and Cassius Dio. The accounts agree on the following picture.

**The funeral oration and display of the body.** Antony, as consul, delivered the funeral oration in the Forum. According to Appian, he read aloud the senate's decrees that had heaped divine and human honors on Caesar, turning toward the corpse with each appellation and adding remarks full of grief and indignation — for instance, noting that the man declared sacred and inviolable had been killed [4]. Cassius Dio records Antony's speech at length, describing the body brought into the Forum “covered with blood as it was and with gaping wounds” and quoting Antony's dramatic lament over the “blood-bespattered locks of gray” and the “rent robe” [2][11]. Plutarch writes that Antony, at the close of his speech, held up the under-clothes of the dead, showing the stains of blood and holes from the many stabs, and called the assassins “villains and bloody murderers” [3][5]. Suetonius adds that instead of a formal panegyric, Antony had a herald proclaim the senate's decree of honors and the oath sworn for Caesar's protection, and added only a few words of his own [8].

**The pyre.** The crowd, inflamed by the speech, refused to wait for the formal funeral procession. They tore up benches, tables, and anything at hand from the surrounding shops and built a funeral pile right in the Forum. Plutarch notes that the spot was surrounded by many temples, so that the burning “seemed to burn the body in a kind of sacred solemnity” [5]. Suetonius records that two men with swords and spears “set fire to the bier with lighted torches,” whereupon the throng heaped on dry faggots, tribunals, and benches. Musicians and players stripped off the triumphal dresses they were wearing and threw them into the flames, and veteran legionaries cast in their armor [8][9]. Ladies threw in ornaments and children's bullae and mantles, and a multitude of foreigners, especially Jews, joined the public mourning [9]. Cassius Dio adds that some wanted to burn the body in the Capitol or in Pompey's senate house, but were prevented by soldiers who feared the temples and theatres would also catch fire; the pyre was therefore erected in the Forum, and even so many of the surrounding buildings would have been destroyed had soldiers not intervened [11]. Appian similarly reports that the people had first tried to bury the body in the temple on the Capitol and were turned away by the priests [1].

**What the funeral unleashed.** The aftermath was immediate and violent. As soon as the pyre was lit, the crowd snatched burning brands and ran through the city to fire the houses of the conspirators [5][10]. According to Plutarch, Brutus and his party had already fortified themselves and repelled the danger, but they soon fled the city altogether [3][5].

The enraged mob, seeking out the praetor Cornelius Cinna (one of the assassins), mistakenly seized the tribune Helvius Cinna, a friend of Caesar who had no part in the conspiracy, and murdered him — parading his head on a spear through the city [11][10].

After the pyre had burned, Caesar's freedmen gathered his bones and deposited them in the family tomb [14]. In the Forum, on the site of the pyre, the populace erected an altar and began to offer sacrifices and victims to Caesar as to a god. The consuls subsequently overturned this altar and punished some who showed displeasure, and passed a law that no one should ever again be named dictator [14]. Suetonius adds that the people later erected in the Forum a column of Numidian marble nearly twenty feet high, inscribed “TO THE FATHER OF HIS COUNTRY,” at which they continued for a long time to sacrifice, make vows, and settle disputes by swearing by Caesar [10].

results.question: What did ancient writers believe about the sources and flooding of the Nile?
results.ideal_answer: Herodotus reviews three Greek theories of the summer flood — the Etesian winds, the world-encircling Ocean, and melting snow — refutes them all (offering his own sun-driven explanation), and reports that no Egyptian, Libyan, or Greek professed to know the river's sources except a Sais scribe he didn't believe. Strabo, writing centuries later, says what the ancients only conjectured had become eyewitness knowledge: the Nile owes its rise to summer rains falling in the mountains of Upper Ethiopia. Ammianus, the latest voice, rehearses the same snow-melt, Etesian-wind and Ethiopian-rain theories but judges the river's sources as unknowable to posterity as they were in his own day.

results.question: How do the sources portray the reign and death of Herod the Great?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus gives the fullest portrait. In the Antiquities he records the grand works of the reign — in his eighteenth year Herod "undertook a very great work, that is, to build of himself the temple of God" — alongside his implacable cruelty, and then the loathsome final illness in which "his privy-member was putrefied, and produced worms," read as divine punishment. The parallel narrative in the Wars closes the reign with the bare notice that, having survived the killing of his son by only five days, Herod died, "having reigned thirty-four years."
results.answer:
The two principal sources in the corpus that portray the reign and death of Herod the Great are **Josephus** (in both his *Antiquities of the Jews* and *The Wars of the Jews*) and **Strabo** (in his *Geography*). Josephus provides a richly detailed, morally complex narrative; Strabo gives a briefer but independent summary.

**Josephus’s Ambivalent Portrait of Herod’s Reign**

Josephus opens his character assessment by noting that observers are amazed at the “diversity of Herod’s nature.” On the one hand, his magnificent building projects and benefactions make it impossible to deny that he had “a nature vastly beneficent”; on the other, his savage punishments and injuries to his own relatives reveal a man “brutish, and a stranger to all humanity.” Josephus himself argues that both sides spring from a single cause: Herod was “a man ambitious of honour,” driven to lavish spending to secure a glorious memorial, and consequently forced to be harsh to his subjects to finance it [1]. At the end of the *Antiquities*, Josephus reiterates this verdict: “A man he was of great barbarity towards all men equally, and a slave to his passion; but above the consideration of what was right; yet was he favoured by fortune as much as any man ever was” [8].

**Building Projects and External Magnificence**

Josephus catalogues Herod’s extraordinary building programme, undertaken both for glory and to cow his subjects. He fortified Samaria, renaming it Sebaste, and erected a magnificent temple there [35]. He turned the old Strato’s Tower into the port-city of Caesarea, complete with a massive artificial harbour, a theatre, an amphitheatre, and a temple to Rome and Caesar [42][36]. He built fortresses such as Herodium, Alexandrium, and Hyrcania, and surrounded the nation with garrisons so that any commotion could be instantly detected [37]. He also rebuilt the Temple at Jerusalem, though Josephus notes that he did this partly out of ambition for a monument of his magnificence [35]. He erected cities named after his father, mother, and brother [38], and celebrated lavish quinquennial games at Caesarea [34]. Yet, as Josephus explains, these displays were financed by harsh exactions: “as his expenses were beyond his abilities, he was necessitated to be harsh to his subjects” [1]. He transgressed Jewish customs by erecting pagan temples outside Judaea, apologising that it was done to please Caesar and the Romans [40].

**Family Murders and Tyranny**

Herod’s domestic cruelty is a constant theme. Josephus records that he slew the principal men of Antigonus’s party [3], and later executed his wife Mariamne, her brother Aristobulus, her mother Alexandra, his sons Aristobulus and Alexander, and eventually a third son, Antipater [6][2]. He was so suspicious that “if any one was not very obsequious to him … he prosecuted his very kindred and friends, and punished them as if they were enemies” [1]. After his death, Jewish ambassadors before Augustus denounced him as a tyrant who “did never leave off adorning these cities that lay in their neighbourhood … but so that the cities belonging to his own government were ruined” and who slew nobles to confiscate their estates [10].

**Strabo’s Independent Summary**

Strabo, writing as a geographer, offers a succinct but independent account. He notes that Herod was an Idumaean “of the same family” who “surrepitiously obtained the priesthood” and so distinguished himself in his dealings with the Romans that he received the title of king, first from Antony, then from Augustus [45]. Strabo adds that Herod “put to death some of his sons, on the pretext of their having conspired against him,” and at his death left other sons to succeed him, with Caesar apportioning the kingdom [45]. This aligns with Josephus on the key points of Roman favour and family bloodshed.

**Herod’s Horrific Death**

Both of Josephus’s works describe the king’s final illness in lurid detail. In the *Antiquities*, Josephus reports that “a fire glowed in him slowly,” his entrails were ulcerated, his colon was in violent pain, his feet swelled with a transparent liquid, his genitals putrefied and bred worms, his breath stank, and convulsions seized his whole body. The diviners, he says, pronounced that “God inflicted this punishment on the king on account of his great impiety” [17][12]. The *Wars* give a similar description, adding that he suffered “an intolerable itching over all the surface of his body” and that the diviners said the diseases were a punishment for what he had done to the Rabbins [13].

**Deathbed Atrocities and Suicide Attempt**

Even as he lay dying, Herod’s barbarity remained. In the *Antiquities*, Josephus recounts that Herod summoned the principal men of the whole Jewish nation, shut them in the hippodrome, and commanded his sister Salome to have them all shot with darts as soon as he died, so that there would be a real mourning at his funeral rather than mockery — a command that, mercifully, was not carried out [4]. Both narratives describe how the king, overwhelmed by pain and lack of food, tried to stab himself with a paring-knife, but was prevented by his cousin Achiabus [7]. He then had Antipater slain, altered his will to make Archelaus his successor, and died five days later [7][8].

In sum, Josephus paints Herod as a man of extraordinary ambition, magnificent public works, and lavish loyalty to Rome, but also of ruthless cruelty, profound insecurity, and a domestic life drenched in blood. His death is portrayed as a divinely sent, grotesque torment — a fitting end for a life of impiety and tyranny. Strabo, more succinctly, confirms the outline of Roman-backed kingship, the murder of his sons, and the posthumous division of his kingdom.

results.question: How do the sources describe Vespasian's rise to the throne during the civil war?
results.ideal_answer: The sources agree that Vespasian was raised to the throne by the armies of the East while the Jewish war was still in progress. Josephus, present in his camp, says he "desire[d] to obtain that government, in order to corroborate his attempts upon the whole empire," and was proclaimed by the soldiers in Judaea and Egypt. Tacitus reports the news reaching the East — "messengers from Syria and Judaea brought the news that the provinces of the East had sworn allegiance to him." Suetonius records the acclamation against Vitellius: the troops in Moesia and Pannonia revolted, "as did likewise, of the armies beyond sea, those in Judaea and Syria," who swore allegiance to Vespasian as emperor.
results.answer:
The sources present Vespasian's rise to the throne during the civil war of AD 69 as a combination of personal reluctance, soldierly initiative, strategic calculation, and key allies. Here is how the major ancient historians describe it:

**Omens and Portents Foretelling His Rule**

Suetonius relates numerous omens that long foreshadowed Vespasian's destiny. On a Flavian family estate near Rome, an old oak sacred to Mars put out a new branch each time Vespasia gave birth — the first withered quickly (her daughter who died young), the second was vigorous (her other daughter), but the third "grew like a tree," pointing to Vespasian's greatness. His father Sabinus told his mother that her grandson would be emperor, to which she merely laughed [12]. Other signs included a stray dog bringing a man's hand under his dinner table, an ox breaking into his house and bowing at his feet, and a cypress-tree that was torn up by the roots only to rise again fresher than before. In Achaia he dreamt that his family's fortune would begin when Nero had a tooth drawn — and the very next day a surgeon showed him Nero's extracted tooth [11]. In Judaea, the oracle of Carmel assured him of success in any undertaking, and the captured Jewish commander Josephus prophesied that Vespasian would soon release him — but would first become emperor [11]. Cassius Dio adds that cosmic omens attended Vitellius's rule: a comet, a double eclipse of the moon, two suns appearing simultaneously — one pale in the west and one brilliant in the east — huge footprints on the Capitol, and the temple of Jupiter opening with a great clangour, all while Vespasian was campaigning in Judaea [8].

**Initial Reluctance**

Though the omens were abundant, Vespasian did not seize the initiative himself. Suetonius says he "made no attempt upon the sovereignty, though his friends were very ready to support him, and even pressed him to the enterprise" [11]. Cassius Dio emphasises his cautious nature: "Vespasian was in general not rashly inclined and he hesitated very much about involving himself in such troublous affairs" [8]. Tacitus describes him as having "no wish or feeling adverse to Galba" and notes that he had sent his son Titus to acknowledge Galba's authority [4].

**The Soldiers Take the Lead**

Two thousand men from three Moesian legions, sent to support Otho, learned of his defeat while on the march; continuing to Aquileia, they plundered the countryside and, fearing punishment, resolved to create their own emperor. After considering the consular lieutenants, soldiers of the third legion (recently transferred from Syria to Moesia) praised Vespasian, and his name was inscribed on their standards. This immediate attempt was temporarily quashed [2].

The decisive momentum came from Egypt. Tiberius Alexander, the governor, acting on his own initiative (or, in Josephus's version, after receiving a letter from Vespasian [3]), administered the oath of allegiance to Vespasian on the first of July — a day "observed ever after as the day of his accession" [2]. Tacitus confirms: "The initiative in transferring the Empire to Vespasian was taken at Alexandria … who on the 1st of July made the legions swear allegiance to him" [c20334].

**The Proclamation in Judaea**

Sources differ on the date. Tacitus reports that the army of Judaea swore allegiance to Vespasian in person "on July 3rd … with such eager alacrity that they would not wait for the return of his son Titus" [c20334]. Suetonius gives the fifth of the ides (July 28) [2]. Tacitus provides the most vivid scene: while supporters were seeking the right moment and the first man bold enough to speak, "one day, on Vespasian quitting his chamber, a few soldiers who stood near … suddenly saluted him as Emperor. Then all the rest hurried up, called him Caesar and Augustus, and heaped on him all the titles of Imperial rank" [1]. Vespasian showed "no sign of elation or arrogance," and after the shock passed, "he addressed the troops in a soldier-like style" [1]. Cassius Dio presents a similar picture: "Vespasian's soldiers on ascertaining all these facts surrounded his tent and hailed him as emperor" [8].

**Mucianus as the Key Facilitator**

Licinius Mucianus, governor of Syria, had previously harboured jealousy toward Vespasian. But Suetonius says he "dropping the grudge … promised to join him with the Syrian army" [7]. Cassius Dio notes that Mucianus urged Vespasian strongly, "hoping that Vespasian should get the name of emperor and that he as a result of the other's good nature should enjoy an equal share of power" [8]. Tacitus underlines that Mucianus "would find it easier to transfer the imperial power to another, than to hold it for himself" [4]. After the soldiers proclaimed Vespasian, Mucianus immediately administered the oath and addressed the people at the theatre in Antioch, speaking Greek elegantly and rousing the provincials by claiming that Vitellius planned to transfer the German legions to Syria's harsh climate [1].

**Securing Egypt and the Grain Supply**

Josephus provides the most detailed account of Vespasian's strategic thinking. After the proclamation, Vespasian's first priority was to gain control of Alexandria, because Egypt was the empire's granary. If he controlled its corn, "he hoped to dethrone Vitellius … for he would not be able to support himself, if the multitude at Rome should once be in want of food" [6]. Josephus also describes Egypt's natural fortifications in detail, explaining why Vespasian saw it as a secure base. Cassius Dio concurs: Vespasian proceeded to Egypt where "he collected money … and grain, which he desired to send in as large quantities as possible to Rome" [8]. Tacitus likewise reports that after the victory at Cremona, Vespasian hastened to Alexandria "to apply the pressure of famine to the capital" and was preparing to invade Africa to close off all corn supplies [15].

**Mucianus Sent to Italy and the Role of Antonius Primus**

Vespasian himself went to Syria and then Egypt; he sent Mucianus to Italy with a considerable army to confront Vitellius. But before Mucianus arrived, the soldiers in Moesia and Pannonia chose Antonius Primus as their general and launched their own offensive. Cassius Dio states they "would not wait for Mucianus" but elected Primus, motivated by "anger at Vitellius and their zest for plunder" [17]. Josephus, who also narrates this episode, reports that Primus took the third legion and marched against Vitellius, while Vitellius's commander Caecina was persuaded (by Primus's overtures) to attempt to betray his own army [18].

Thus, across the sources, Vespasian's rise appears as a convergence of soldierly impulse, the relinquishment of personal rivalry by Mucianus, the strategic seizure of Egypt's grain, and the rapid coalition of eastern legions — all against the backdrop of omens that both contemporaries and later writers wove into the narrative of his destined rule.

results.question: How do the sources tell the rise and fall of Theban supremacy under Epaminondas?
results.ideal_answer: The sources trace Theban hegemony as the personal achievement of Epaminondas — and its collapse with his death. Xenophon, contemporary and cool toward Thebes, ends his history on the inconclusive note that after Mantinea "the effective result of these achievements was the very opposite of that which the world at large anticipated." Diodorus makes the dependence explicit: while Epaminondas lived "he gained the sovereign power for his country; but, by his death they lost it again" — "and this was the end of Epaminondas, a man honoured and esteemed of all." Pausanias records his monument, whose elegiac lines tell "how he restored Messene, and how the Greeks got their freedom through him." Plutarch supplies the Theban view of the bond with Pelopidas, for whom Epaminondas "singly fought a multitude, resolving rather to die than forsake his helpless" friend.
results.answer:
The sources portray Theban supremacy under Epaminondas as a meteoric phenomenon—rising abruptly from the battle of Leuctra (371 BC), reaching its peak with the invasion of Laconia and the founding of Megalopolis and Messene, and collapsing instantly with Epaminondas's death at Mantinea (362 BC). The narrative is drawn principally from Xenophon's *Hellenica*, Diodorus Siculus's *Historical Library*, Plutarch's *Life of Pelopidas*, and Pausanias's *Description of Greece*, with scholarly synthesis from J. B. Bury.

### The Rise: Leuctra and the Shattering of Spartan Prestige

Before Leuctra, Thebes had been recovering its strength through a series of skirmishes with Spartan forces in Boeotia—encounters that, in Plutarch's words, "had their minds raised, and their bodies inured to labour, and gained both experience and courage" [22]. When the Spartan king Cleombrotus invaded Boeotia with ten thousand foot and one thousand horse, Pelopidas threw his support behind Epaminondas, who argued for battle [6]. Xenophon records that Cleombrotus marched through Thisbae by an unexpected mountain route, seized the fortress of Creusis and twelve Theban warships, then encamped at Leuctra [13].

The battle itself was a tactical revolution. Epaminondas massed his Theban hoplites in a deep column on the left wing—the so-called "oblique phalanx"—and drove through the Spartan right, where Cleombrotus and the élite Spartiates were stationed. Diodorus reports that the Lacedaemonians lost no fewer than four thousand men, while the Boeotians lost only about three hundred [10]. Pausanias gives the Spartan dead at more than one thousand, against forty-seven Boeotian dead [26]. Xenophon, describing the aftermath, notes that of the seven hundred Spartans on the field, around four hundred lay dead; the polemarchs found the allies despondent, some "not far removed from positive satisfaction at what had taken place" [16]. The Thebans allowed the Lacedaemonians to bury their dead only after the allies had buried theirs—thereby exposing that the vast majority of fallen were Spartans [26].

Bury emphasizes that the disease afflicting Sparta was deeper than a single defeat: "at this time there were not more than 1500 with full citizenship" [32]. Plutarch, in the *Life of Agesilaus*, remarks that "this unexpected blow… brought greater glory to Thebes than ever was acquired by any other of the Grecian republics, in their civil wars against each other" [7].

### The Peak: Invasion of Laconia and the Reordering of the Peloponnese

In the winter following Leuctra, Epaminondas led a massive allied army into the Peloponnese. Xenophon describes his actual entry into the city of Sparta, where he selected a position and began advancing downhill; the Spartans, led by Archidamus with only a hundred men, managed to repel the Theban vanguard in what Xenophon calls a "miraculous" turn—though the pursuing Spartans were then cut down in turn [3]. Diodorus adds that Epaminondas sounded a retreat after heavy losses among his men assaulting the city, then challenged the Spartans to come out and fight, which they refused [19].

What Epaminondas did next proved more permanently damaging than any assault on Sparta itself. He persuaded the Arcadians and other allies to refound two cities that would hem Sparta in for generations. Pausanias records that "as the small towns of the Arcadians were insecure, he persuaded the Arcadians to evacuate them, and established for them one large town still called Megalopolis" [29]. Diodorus notes that this occurred after a Spartan victory over ten thousand Arcadians had terrified them into seeking security in a single large city [17].

Even more consequential was the restoration of Messene. Diodorus states that Epaminondas "invited the Messenians from all places where they were, rebuilt Messene, and restored to the new inhabitants all the ancient territories formerly belonging to that city"—accomplishing all this in eighty-five days [18]. Bury observes that the Messenian exiles "who had been wandering over the Greek world had now a home once more" and that the serfs belonging to the old Messenian race "arose" [32]. On Epaminondas's statue, Pausanias notes four elegiac lines recording that "he restored Messene, and how the Greeks got their freedom through him" [27].

Epaminondas also showed political judgment, albeit inconsistently. After Leuctra he persuaded the Thebans to use their victory with moderation and received the Orchomenians as confederates rather than destroying their city [10]. In Achaea, however, he left the aristocratic governments in place—only for the home government at Thebes to expel them and install democracies under harmosts, which drove the Achaean exiles into the arms of Sparta [2]. Diodorus records that Thebes also began building a hundred triremes with docks, and that Epaminondas himself was sent to Rhodes, Chios, and Byzantium, reducing those cities to Theban obedience and terrifying the Athenian general Laches into retreat; Diodorus adds that "if this man had lived some time longer, he had gained for the Thebans the sovereign command both at sea and land" [8].

### The Fall: Mantinea and the Death of Epaminondas

The supremacy of Thebes proved entirely dependent on one man. In 362 BC, faced with a coalition of Spartans, Athenians, Mantineans, and others, Epaminondas marched into the Peloponnese once more. Bury details his tactics at Mantinea: he again placed the Boeotian hoplites in a deep column on the left, with cavalry massed in front, and sent a detachment to occupy a rise in the plain to prevent the Athenians on the enemy left from flanking his column [33]. Diodorus describes the fighting: "while he was most heroically exerting himself to gain the victory for his country, he received a mortal wound in his breast by a dart, thrown with such force, as that the wood broke, and the iron, with the truncheon, remained in his body" [12].

Carried back to camp alive, Epaminondas asked first whether his shield was safe and which side had won. Told that the Boeotians were victorious, he declared "Now is the time to die" and ordered the dart drawn out [11]. Pausanias adds the detail that "for a time he kept his hand on his wound, and gasped for breath, and looked earnestly at the fight, and the place where he kept so looking they called ever after Scope (Watch)" [28]. Diodorus preserves his most famous parting words: when friends lamented that he died childless, he answered, "I leave behind me two daughters, whereof the one is Victory at Leuctra, and the other at Mantinea" [11].

The collapse was immediate. Diodorus states bluntly: "by his death they lost it again, and their affairs declined to the worse ever afterwards; and at length, by the sloth and ignorance of their commanders, they were utterly ruined, and reduced to perfect slavery" [9]. Elsewhere he writes that "all the prosperity of the Thebans presently died (as it were) with him" [8]. Bury concurs, observing that "his political faculty was mediocre… he failed to solve the fundamental problems which demanded solution at the hands of a statesman who aimed at making his country great" and that the new constitution "had no elements of stability, and it endured but for a few years" [30]. The general peace that followed at once included the Messenians but excluded the Spartans, who refused to swear to it because of their implacable hatred of the restored Messenians [9].

results.question: What period of history did Gibbon declare the happiest and most prosperous for the human race?
results.ideal_answer: Gibbon wrote that if asked to fix the period during which the condition of the human race was most happy and prosperous, one would without hesitation name the span from the death of Domitian to the accession of Commodus — the era of Nerva, Trajan, Hadrian, and the Antonines.

results.question: According to Tacitus, what did the Germans consider the greatest disgrace in battle?
results.ideal_answer: Tacitus says the greatest disgrace that could befall a German warrior was to have abandoned his shield. He adds that for a chief's companions it was lifelong reproach and infamy to retreat from the field surviving their chief.

results.question: Whom did the Athenians send as a runner to Sparta before the battle of Marathon, and what happened to him on the way?
