results.question: What epitaph was inscribed for the Spartans who fell at Thermopylae?
results.ideal_answer: The inscription for the Spartans read (in Macaulay's translation): "Stranger, report this word, we pray, to the Spartans, that lying here in this spot we remain, faithfully keeping their laws."

results.question: How does Caesar describe the division of Gaul at the start of his commentaries?
results.ideal_answer: Caesar opens by stating that all Gaul is divided into three parts, one inhabited by the Belgae, another by the Aquitani, and the third by the people called Celts in their own language and Gauls in Latin — all differing from each other in language, customs, and laws.
results.answer: Caesar opens his commentaries on the Gallic War by stating that Gaul as a whole is divided into three parts: one inhabited by the Belgae, another by the Aquitani, and the third by those who call themselves Celts but whom the Romans call Gauls. He adds that all three groups differ from one another in language, customs, and laws. The river Garonne separates the Gauls from the Aquitani, while the Marne and the Seine separate them from the Belgae. He also notes that the Belgae are the bravest of the three, being farthest from Roman civilization and closest to the Germans, with whom they are constantly at war [2].

results.question: What answer did the Delphic oracle give Croesus when he asked whether to attack Persia?

results.question: How many Jews perished and how many were taken captive during the whole siege of Jerusalem in the Jewish War?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus reports that the number carried captive during the whole war was ninety-seven thousand, and the number who perished during the whole siege was eleven hundred thousand (1,100,000) — most of them Jews who had come up from the country to Jerusalem for the feast of unleavened bread and were then shut in by the Roman army, destroyed by pestilence and famine.

results.question: What did Eleazar urge the defenders of Masada to do rather than fall into Roman hands?
results.ideal_answer: Eleazar urged the defenders to die free by their own hands rather than be taken — to slay their own wives and children and then one another, having first burned their possessions and the fortress, so as to preserve their freedom and deny the Romans the pleasure of capturing them. They carried out the mass suicide, sparing only their stores of food.

He told them to kill their wives before they could be abused and their children before they experienced slavery, then to draw lots and kill one another so that they would die free. He also commanded them to burn their money and the fortress, sparing only their provisions as a testament that they had not been starved into submission, but had chosen death over slavery [1]. The speech persuaded most, and they carried out the grim plan, with only two women and five children surviving by hiding [2].

results.question: What act of a starving mother during the famine in Jerusalem does Josephus describe in the Wars of the Jews?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus describes Mary of Bethezuba, a once-wealthy woman driven to madness by the famine, who killed her infant son, roasted him, ate one half and kept the other half concealed — an atrocity he says had no parallel in Greek or barbarian history.

Besieged and driven to extremity by famine and the rapacity of the seditious guards who had stripped her of all her provisions [3], she snatched up her infant son, slew him, roasted his body, ate one half, and concealed the remaining half [3][1]. When the seditious guards later came, drawn by the smell of cooking, she uncovered the remains and dared them to eat, saying she had already consumed her portion [1].

results.question: What was the supreme or national god of the Assyrians?
results.ideal_answer: The supreme, distinctively national god of the Assyrians was Asshur — head of the Assyrian pantheon, styled "the great Lord" and "King of all the Gods," the tutelary deity of the kings and of the country, whose name was identical with that of Assyria itself.
results.answer: The supreme national god of the Assyrians was Asshur. According to George Rawlinson's "Seven Great Monarchies, Vol. 2 — Assyria," Asshur stood at the head of the Assyrian Pantheon with titles such as "the great Lord," "the King of all the Gods," and "he who rules supreme over the Gods" [1]. He was regarded as the especial tutelary deity of the kings and the country, and his worship was widespread, as indicated by the lack of a single localized temple [1]. The Assyrians were frequently described as "the servants of Asshur," and their religion as "the worship of Asshur" [2].

results.question: What was the principal building material of Chaldaea, and why was stone so little used?
results.ideal_answer: The principal building material of Chaldaea was brick, made from the abundant clay of the alluvial plain (sun-dried, or kiln-baked for greater hardness). Stone was scarcely used because the alluvium was destitute of it — any stone had to be imported from adjacent countries — so the Chaldaeans built with clay brick in its default.
results.answer: The principal building material of Chaldaea was brick, both sun-dried and kiln-baked, made from the excellent and inexhaustible clay of the region [1][3]. Stone was little used because the Chaldaean alluvium was almost entirely devoid of stone, with no quarries within its limits [1][3]. Although stone could be imported from neighboring areas or brought down by the rivers, such importation was very limited, as the soft and oozy soil of the plain made transporting heavy stone extremely difficult, especially in early times before numerous canals were constructed [1][3]. Consequently, the Chaldaeans were forced to rely on brick as a durable substitute for the stone employed by most other nations [3].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the cited source passages, with no invented content or unsupported embellishment. | completeness: The answer fully addresses both parts of the question by identifying brick as the principal material and explaining that stone was scarce locally and difficult to transport, leaving no required information missing. | attribution: The cited sources agree on the principal building material and the reasons for the limited use of stone, and the answer correctly attributes the synthesized details using bare citation markers without any misattribution.

results.question: What did Maharbal say to Hannibal after the battle of Cannae?
results.ideal_answer: When Hannibal hesitated to march at once on Rome after Cannae, his cavalry commander Maharbal — who had urged that within five days Hannibal might dine in the Capitol — rebuked him: "You know how to conquer, Hannibal, but you do not know how to make use of your victory." Livy adds that this day's delay is believed to have saved the city and the empire.
results.answer:
After the battle of Cannae, Maharbal, Hannibal's cavalry commander, urged him to march on Rome immediately, saying that in five days he could feast in triumph in the Capitol. When Hannibal hesitated, saying he needed time to consider, Maharbal replied: **"Of a truth the gods have not bestowed all things upon the same person. You know how to conquer, Hannibal; but you do not know how to make use of your victory."** [1]

Plutarch records a very similar version, though he names the speaker "Barcas, a Carthaginian" (likely a variant or corruption of Maharbal): **"You know, Hannibal, how to gain a victory, but not how to use it."** [19]

Polybius, whose account of Cannae is detailed, does not include this anecdote at all.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: While the quotations from Livy and Plutarch are accurate and well-cited, the answer adds an unsupported speculation that "Barcas" is a variant or corruption of Maharbal, which appears in none of the provided passages. | completeness: The answer fully quotes Maharbal's rebuke and provides the relevant context about marching on Rome, completely addressing what the question asks. | attribution: The answer correctly attributes the quotes to Livy and Plutarch in prose, accurately noting and explaining the slight variation in the speaker's name between the two sources.

results.question: What did Scipio Africanus say to the crowd on the anniversary of his victory when prosecuted, before leading them to the Capitol?
results.ideal_answer: Brought to trial on the very anniversary of the battle, Scipio reminded the assembly that on that day he had fought and beaten Hannibal and the Carthaginians in Africa (at Zama); declaring it unfit to spend such a day in litigation, he went up to the Capitol to thank the gods — and the whole assembly left the tribunes and followed him.
results.answer:
On the anniversary of his victory at Zama over Hannibal, when Scipio Africanus was prosecuted by the tribunes and arrived at the rostrum for the second day of his trial, he addressed the crowd as follows [3]:

> “Tribunes of the people, and you, Romans, on the anniversary of this day I fought a pitched battle in Africa, with Hannibal and the Carthaginians, with good fortune and success. As, therefore, it is but decent that a stop be put, for this day, to litigation and wrangling, I will immediately go to the Capitol, there to return my acknowledgments to Jupiter the supremely good and great, to Juno, Minerva, and the other deities presiding over the Capitol and citadel, and will give them thanks for having, on this day, and at many other times, endowed me both with the will and ability to perform extraordinary services to the commonwealth. Such of you also, Romans, as it suits, come with me and beseech the gods that you may have commanders like myself; since, from my seventeenth year to old age, you have always anticipated my years with honours, and I, your honours with services.”

After this speech, he went up from the rostrum to the Capitol, and the entire assembly turned and followed him; even the clerks and messengers abandoned the tribunes, leaving only their slaves and the crier [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The quoted speech, the context of the second day of trial, and the aftermath of the crowd following Scipio to the Capitol are all fully supported by passages [2] and [3], with no fabricated or unsupported claims. | completeness: The answer fully covers what Scipio said, the anniversary and prosecution context, and his leading the crowd to the Capitol, addressing every element the question asks for. | attribution: The source passages agree and complement each other, and the answer correctly attributes the quotes and subsequent events to the appropriate sources without any misattribution.

results.question: What city did Alexander found in memory of the horse he had tamed as a boy?
results.ideal_answer: Bucephala, on the Hydaspes. As a boy Alexander tamed the horse Bucephalas (turning him toward the sun after noticing he feared his own shadow); when the horse died of age and toil after the battle with Porus, Alexander founded the city of Bucephala in his memory.
results.answer: Alexander founded the city of Bucephala (also spelled Bucephalia) in memory of his horse Bucephalus. Arrian reports that after the battle with Porus at the Hydaspes River, Alexander founded two cities: Nicaea for his victory, and Bucephala "in memory of his horse Bucephalas, which died there, not from having been wounded by any one, but from the effects of toil and old age" [1]. results.question: How did the wife of the emperor who added Britain to the empire meet her end?
results.ideal_answer: The emperor is Claudius, who invaded Britain to gain a triumph; his wife Messalina, after publicly marrying her lover Silius, was ordered to answer for her conduct, failed in her attempts at suicide in the gardens of Lucullus, and was run through with a sword by a tribune.
results.answer: The emperor who added Britain to the empire was Claudius. His wife Messalina was executed in the gardens of Lucullus. According to Tacitus, when a tribune and soldiers arrived, she put a dagger to her throat and breast ineffectually, and then the tribune drove the dagger through her [9]. Suetonius records that a tribune burst into the gardens and plunged his sword into her body, killing her instantly [7]. Cassius Dio adds that she was slain after retreating into the gardens of Asiaticus [8]. Claudius later married Agrippina the Younger, who survived him and was murdered in 59 CE on the orders of her son Nero. After surviving a shipwreck plot, Agrippina was killed at her home; Cassius Dio reports that Nero sent Anicetus and sailors to kill her, and when she bared her abdomen, she was slain [1]. Tacitus gives a similar account: the captain of the trireme struck her head with a club, and a centurion then killed her with multiple wounds, as she cried, 'Smite my womb' [2].

results.question: Why did the Greek army Xenophon served with have to fight its way home?
results.ideal_answer: Their employer Cyrus the Younger was killed at Cunaxa charging his brother King Artaxerxes (struck by a javelin under the eye), leaving the Greeks victorious but stranded deep in Persia; then Tissaphernes treacherously seized their five generals at a conference and cut down the captains, leaving the Ten Thousand leaderless and forced to fight their way to the sea.
results.answer: The Greek army Xenophon served with — the Ten Thousand — had to fight its way home because, after their employer Cyrus the Younger was killed at the Battle of Cunaxa [6][2], the Persians treacherously murdered their generals. Tissaphernes negotiated a truce and promised to lead the Greeks safely back to Hellas [15], but then invited five generals (including Clearchus, Proxenus, Menon, Agias, and Socrates) and twenty captains to his quarters, where the generals were seized and the captains cut down [10]. The captured generals were taken to the king and decapitated [9]. The surviving Greeks were left stranded deep in Persian territory — surrounded by hostile peoples, without a market or a guide, and hemmed in by impassable rivers — and were forced to fight their way home [4][11].

results.question: How did Alexander react on finding the tomb of the founder of the Persian empire desecrated?
results.ideal_answer: Finding the tomb of Cyrus (whose epitaph read "I am Cyrus, son of Cambyses, who founded the empire of the Persians") dug through and pillaged at Pasargadae, Alexander was grieved; he commissioned Aristobulus to restore the tomb and its furnishings, seal the entrance, and he had the Magian guards arrested and tortured to find the culprits.
results.answer:
When Alexander discovered that the tomb of Cyrus the Great at Pasargadae had been desecrated, he was deeply grieved and took several actions to address the outrage [3].

According to Arrian's account (drawing on Aristobulus, who was personally involved in the restoration), Alexander found the tomb pillaged: everything except the coffin and couch had been carried off, the lid of the coffin had been torn off, the body cast out, and the coffin itself partly cut and crushed in an unsuccessful attempt to make it portable [1]. In response, Alexander commissioned Aristobulus himself to restore the tomb — replacing the body's preserved parts in the coffin, restoring the damaged coffin, re-stretching the couch with bands, depositing replacement ornaments matching the originals, and sealing the entrance with stone and cement bearing the royal seal [1]. Alexander also arrested the Magian guards and put them to torture to extract a confession, but they revealed nothing and were ultimately released [1][2].

Plutarch, however, offers a different account of the punishment. He writes that Alexander found the sepulchre opened and rifled, and put a Macedonian nobleman named Polymachus to death for the act [6]. Plutarch adds that, after reading the tomb's famous inscription — “O man, whosoever thou art, and from whencesoever thou comest … I am Cyrus, the founder of the Persian empire; do not grudge me this little earth which covers my body” — Alexander was sensibly touched, filled with thoughts of the uncertainty and mutability of human affairs, and ordered the inscription to be re-cut below the old one in Greek characters [6].

Thus the sources agree that Alexander was distressed by the violation and took steps to restore and re-secure the tomb, but they differ on who was held responsible: Arrian credits him with torturing (but releasing) the Magian guards, while Plutarch says he executed Polymachus.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided source passages, with no invented content or unsupported embellishment. | completeness: The answer fully covers Alexander's emotional grief, his commissioning of Aristobulus to restore and seal the tomb, and his punitive actions against the guards, while also noting source variations, thereby addressing all aspects of the question. | attribution: The answer correctly identifies and surfaces the disagreement between Arrian and Plutarch regarding the punishment for the tomb's desecration, clearly attributing each distinct version to its respective source in prose.

results.question: Which Spartan commanded the Greeks at the land battle fought the same day as the sea battle at Mycale?
results.ideal_answer: Pausanias, son of Cleombrotos (regent for Leonidas' young son Pleistarchos). Herodotus reports that the victory at Plataea and the fight at Mycale fell on the same day, and that Pausanias was appointed to lead the Spartan army that won at Plataea.
results.answer: The Spartan commander at the land battle fought on the same day as the sea battle at Mycale was Pausanias, son of Cleombrotos. This refers to the Battle of Plataea, which, according to a famous tradition recorded by Herodotus, took place on the very same day as the battle at Mycale [14]. Herodotus explicitly states that 'the most famous victory of all ... was gained by Pausanias the son of Cleombrotos' at Plataea [9].

results.question: How did the statesman who championed Athens' Long Walls die?
results.ideal_answer: Pericles — who, as Socrates attested, proposed the long wall to the people — died of the plague of Athens, in his case not with violent fits but as a dull, lingering distemper that slowly wasted his body and mind.
results.answer: The statesman who championed the Long Walls was Themistocles. He persuaded the Athenians to fortify the Piraeus and to build the walls connecting Athens to the sea [11][19][13]. He died in Magnesia. The sources give conflicting accounts of his death. Thucydides states that disease was the real cause, although there is a story that he ended his life by poison when he saw he could not fulfill his promises to the Persian king [6]. Plutarch reports that Themistocles, unwilling to act against the Greeks, sacrificed and drank bull's blood, a deadly poison, thus taking his own life [5]. J. B. Bury explains that the report of suicide by bull's blood was a false story that arose decades later from a misunderstanding of a statue in the Magnesian marketplace that depicted him pouring a libation over a slain bull [2].

results.question: What revolt broke out in Britain under the emperor who was the last of his dynasty?
results.ideal_answer: The revolt of Boudicca (Buduica), queen of the Iceni, broke out under Nero — whom Dio calls the last of the descendants of Aeneas and Augustus. While the governor Paulinus was away at Mona she sacked two Roman cities with enormous slaughter before being defeated.
results.answer: The revolt that broke out in Britain under the last emperor of his dynasty was **the rebellion of Boudica (also spelled Boadicea or Buduica)**, which occurred during the reign of **Nero**, the last emperor of the Julio-Claudian dynasty. According to the sources, Boudica, queen of the Iceni, led a massive uprising after the Romans mistreated her and her people. Cassius Dio reports that the revolt resulted in the sacking of two Roman cities and the slaughter of some eighty thousand Romans and allies [3], and that Boudica commanded an army of about 230,000 men before being defeated in battle by the governor Suetonius Paullinus [1]. Suetonius likewise records that Boadicea, after being treated ignominiously by Roman tribunes, drove out the invaders, burned London (a flourishing Roman colony), and put seventy thousand Romans and strangers to the sword before being defeated in a decisive engagement [2]. Tacitus' *Agricola* also mentions the uprising led by Boadicea, who attacked Roman garrisons and the colony itself, and notes that the province was nearly lost before Paullinus brought it back under subjection in a single battle [4]. Nero was indeed the last of the Julio-Claudian line (Augustus, Tiberius, Caligula, Claudius, Nero).

results.question: How did the brother of Hannibal who marched an army into Italy to reinforce him meet his end?
results.ideal_answer: Hasdrubal, Hannibal's brother, crossed the Alps into Italy to reinforce him but was defeated and killed by the consuls Marcus Livius and Claudius Nero at the river Metaurus (207 BC); his severed head, carried south, was thrown before the outposts of Hannibal's camp.
results.answer:
Hasdrubal Barca, Hannibal's brother, died fighting bravely at the Battle of the Metaurus in 207 BC, and his severed head was thrown into Hannibal's camp by the Romans to announce his defeat.

Livy provides the most detailed account: after fighting valiantly — encouraging his men, calling back the fleeing, and sharing every danger — Hasdrubal saw the battle was lost, "put spurs to his horse and rushed upon a Roman cohort, where he fell fighting, as was worthy of the son of Hamilcar and the brother of Hannibal" [11][10]. Livy also records that the consul Gaius Claudius Nero "ordered the head of Hasdrubal, which he had carefully kept and brought with him, to be thrown before the advanced guards of the enemy" [1].

Polybius concurs, stating that "Hasdrubal had behaved on this occasion, as throughout his whole life, like a brave man, and died fighting" [c6163]. Cassius Dio writes that the Romans "had destroyed Hasdrubal along with many others" and that Nero "fixed Hasdrubal's head on a pole nearby" Hannibal's camp, causing Hannibal to lament bitterly [5]. The scholarly accounts of Mommsen and William Smith likewise emphasize that he sought and found an honorable soldier's death [2][3].

results.question: How did the Carthaginian commander who led elephants across the Alps eventually die, long after his war with Rome?
results.ideal_answer: Hannibal, who led his army with its elephants across the Alps into Italy in the Second Punic War, died decades later in exile: cornered at the court of king Prusias by a Roman demand for his surrender, he drank the poison he had long kept ready.
results.answer:
Hannibal died by suicide in Bithynia, long after the Second Punic War, when he was an old man living as a guest at the court of King Prusias. The Roman envoy Titus Quinctius Flamininus, sent on an unrelated embassy, pressured Prusias to hand Hannibal over. Prusias, fearing Roman displeasure, surrounded Hannibal's house with guards [1][5]. Hannibal had long anticipated such treachery and had prepared seven concealed underground passages from his house, but when he tried to escape he found every exit blocked by the king's soldiers [5][4][1].

There are several ancient accounts of the exact method of his death:

- **Poison**: Livy reports that Hannibal called for poison which he had long kept ready for such an emergency, drank it, and died, uttering final words rebuking the Romans for their faithlessness — contrasting their present conduct with their ancestors who had warned Pyrrhus against poison [1]. Plutarch also records this version, attributing it to Livy [4]. Mommsen and Cassius Dio likewise state that he took poison [2][3].

- **Strangulation**: Plutarch notes that some say Hannibal wrapped his upper garment around his neck and commanded a servant to twist it until he was strangled [4].

- **Bull's blood**: Plutarch also records a tradition that he drank bull's blood, following the example of Themistocles and Midas [4].

Plutarch adds that the Roman senators, upon hearing the news, were divided — some blamed Flamininus for his officious cruelty in hunting down an old man who no longer posed a threat, and they contrasted his conduct unfavourably with the clemency Scipio Africanus had shown Hannibal after defeating him [4].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is otherwise fully grounded, but it misattributes the specific detail of poison to Cassius Dio in passage [3], which only records that Hannibal committed suicide without naming the method. | completeness: The answer fully and correctly covers how Hannibal died long after the war, addressing the circumstances, location, and specific reported methods of his suicide without omitting anything the question asks. | attribution: The answer successfully surfaces the differing historical accounts regarding the exact method of Hannibal's death and clearly attributes each version to its respective source in prose.

results.question: Who commanded the Roman army that razed Carthage to the ground, and what did he reportedly weep over as the city burned?
results.ideal_answer: Scipio Aemilianus (Scipio the Younger), who destroyed Carthage in the Third Punic War (146 BC). As the city burned he shed tears and, asked by Polybius what he meant, admitted he frankly named his own country — fearing that one day the same fate would befall Rome.
results.answer:
The Roman army that razed Carthage was commanded by **Scipio Aemilianus** (also known as Scipio Africanus the Younger) [1][2][5].

As Carthage burned, Scipio reportedly wept over the destruction of the once-great city and reflected on the mutability of human affairs. According to Appian, he "is said to have shed tears and publicly lamented the fortune of the enemy" and, after meditating on the rise and fall of cities and empires, when asked by Polybius what he meant, "he did not hesitate frankly to name his own country, for whose fate he feared when he considered the mutability of human affairs" [1]. Polybius's own account confirms that Scipio watched Carthage burn "with tearful eyes" and uttered "the foreboding of what might one day befall Rome" [5]. William Smith adds that Scipio "is said to have repeated the lines of the Iliad over the flames of Carthage: 'The day shall come when sacred Troy shall perish, and Priam and his people shall be slain'" [2].

results.question: Which Theban general broke Spartan power in a great pitched battle, and where did he fall in his last victory?
results.ideal_answer: Epaminondas of Thebes, who shattered Spartan supremacy at Leuctra (371 BC) and fell mortally wounded at Mantinea (362 BC), dying in the very hour of his victory.
results.answer: The Theban general was Epaminondas. He broke Spartan power at the Battle of Leuctra, where his tactical innovations routed the previously invincible Spartan army [4]. He fell in his last victory at the Battle of Mantinea, where he was mortally wounded by a spear while leading the Theban charge and died after learning his side had won [1][2]. He was buried on the field of battle [3].

results.question: Which Achaean coastal city was swallowed by the sea in a single night, and what was blamed for the catastrophe?
results.ideal_answer: Helike (Helice) in Achaea, which an earthquake and an inundation of the sea overwhelmed in a single night (373 BC). The catastrophe was blamed on the wrath of Poseidon, whose suppliants the people of Helike had dragged from his temple and slain.
results.answer: The Achaean coastal city swallowed by the sea in a single night was Helice (also spelled Helike). According to the ancient sources, the catastrophe was blamed on the anger of the god Poseidon (Neptune). Strabo reports that the inundation happened during the night and that the calamity occurred "in consequence of the anger of Neptune" [2]. Pausanias states that the Achaeans "drove some suppliants from the temple and slew them" and that they "met with quick vengeance from Poseidon, for an earthquake coming over the place rapidly overthrew all the buildings" and the sea then flooded the city [5][3]. Diodorus Siculus also describes the destruction of Helice and Bura, noting that those with "more reverend thoughts and sentiments of a deity" attributed the event to "the anger of the gods for the impious violation of the rights of religion" [1][4].

results.question: How did the Spartan regent who led the Greeks to victory at Plataea fall from power and die?
results.ideal_answer: Pausanias the regent, who commanded the victorious Greeks at Plataea (479 BC), afterwards intrigued with Persia to make himself master of Greece; detected, he fled to the temple of Athena of the Brazen House, where the ephors roofed him in, barricaded the doors and starved him, bringing him out only as he was about to expire.
results.answer:
Pausanias, the Spartan regent who commanded the Greek forces at the victory of Plataea, fell from power through a protracted scandal involving treason with Persia ("Medism") and died a dramatic death by starvation in the temple of Athena Chalkioikos (the Brazen House) in Sparta.

**Initial suspicions and recall from Byzantium**

After Plataea, Pausanias was given command of the allied fleet. His conduct aroused deep suspicion: he behaved arrogantly, imitated Persian customs, and was believed to be intriguing with the Persians. Expelled from Byzantium by the Athenians, he did not return to Sparta but settled at Colonae in the Troad, where he continued his suspected intrigues with the barbarians. The ephors sent a herald with a scytale (the Spartan dispatch-staff) ordering him to return or be declared a public enemy. He complied, was thrown into prison, but "soon compromised the matter and came out again," offering himself for trial — the Spartans lacked the "indubitable kind" of proof needed to punish a member of the royal family [2].

Among other evidence that gained new meaning in hindsight was Pausanias's arrogant inscription on the tripod at Delphi after Plataea, taking personal credit rather than acknowledging the allied Greek cities: "The Mede defeated, great Pausanias raised / This monument, that Phoebus might be praised" — a couplet the Spartans had promptly erased at the time [2]. He was also found to be intriguing with the Helots, promising them freedom and citizenship if they joined an insurrection [2].

**The Argilian messenger and the ephors' proof**

The decisive evidence came through Pausanias's own trusted servant, a man of Argilus. Noticing that none of the previous messengers sent to Persia had ever returned, the Argilian opened the last letter Pausanias entrusted to him and discovered a postscript ordering the bearer's death. He took the letter to the ephors. Still cautious, the ephors devised a stratagem: the Argilian took sanctuary as a suppliant at Taenarus in a hut divided by a partition, behind which some of the ephors concealed themselves. Pausanias came to ask why the man had fled, and in the ensuing conversation admitted the full truth of his treasonable correspondence with Persia [6].

**Death by starvation in the Brazen House**

The ephors now prepared to arrest Pausanias in Sparta. Warned by a signal from one of the ephors, Pausanias ran for sanctuary to the nearby temple of Athena Chalkioikos ("the goddess of the Brazen House"), entered a small chamber within the enclosure, and lay still there. The ephors removed the roof of the chamber, confirmed that he was inside, and then "shut him in, barricaded the doors, and staying before the place, reduced him by starvation." When he was on the point of death, they brought him out — still breathing — and he died the moment he was outside the temple [1].

**The Cleonice story**

The travel writer Pausanias (living centuries later) records an additional tradition he heard at Byzantium: that Pausanias, while commanding the allied fleet at the Hellespont, became enamoured of a Byzantine maiden named Cleonice. When she was brought to his room at night, she accidentally knocked over a lamp; the noise woke Pausanias, whose "conscience smote him for having betrayed Greece, and who was therefore always in a state of nervous alarm and panic," and he fatally stabbed her with a scimitar. This guilt, according to that account, was the reason he could not secure indemnity from the goddess even in sanctuary [5].

**Aftermath**

The Spartans initially planned to throw his body into the Kaiadas (the chasm where criminals were cast), but instead buried him somewhere nearby. Afterward, the oracle at Delphi ordered the Lacedaemonians to move his tomb to the very place of his death — consecrated ground — and, since what they had done was a curse to them, to "give back two bodies instead of one to the goddess of the Brazen House." They accordingly had two brazen statues made and dedicated them as a substitute for Pausanias [1][5].

Plutarch's *Life of Themistocles* adds that after Pausanias's death, letters and writings were found that implicated Themistocles in the treasonable correspondence — though Themistocles had rejected Pausanias's overtures — which contributed to Themistocles's eventual downfall and flight to Persia [4].

results.question: How did the founder of the Persian empire meet his death on campaign?
results.ideal_answer: Cyrus the Great, founder of the Persian empire, was killed on campaign beyond the Araxes against the Massagetae and their queen Tomyris; the greater part of his army was destroyed and Cyrus himself fell there, after a reign of twenty-nine years.
results.answer: The founder of the Persian Empire, Cyrus the Great, died on campaign against the Massagetae, a nomadic people ruled by Queen Tomyris, according to the main account by Herodotus [1]. After a fierce and prolonged battle in which the greater part of the Persian army was destroyed, Cyrus himself fell [1]. Herodotus relates that Tomyris then sought out his corpse, filled a skin with human blood, and dipped his head into it, declaring that she would give him his fill of blood as she had threatened [1][5]. However, later sources note conflicting traditions: the Greek historian Ctesias (as cited by Rawlinson) claimed that Cyrus was mortally wounded in a battle against the Derbices near India, dying of his wound three days afterward [2]. Rawlinson observes that the exact circumstances are uncertain, but it is established that Cyrus died on his north-eastern frontier around 529 B.C. and his body was returned to Persia for burial at Pasargadae [2].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every substantive claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided source passages, with no invented content. | completeness: The answer fully explains how Cyrus the Great died on campaign against the Massagetae and Queen Tomyris, including the destruction of his army and his death in battle, without omitting anything the question requires. | attribution: The answer correctly surfaces the disagreement among the sources regarding Cyrus's death and clearly attributes each distinct account to its respective author in prose.

results.question: Which Jewish prisoner predicted that his Roman captor would become emperor, and did the prophecy come true?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus, captured at Jotapata, predicted to Vespasian that he would become Caesar and emperor. The prophecy came true when the eastern legions proclaimed Vespasian emperor (AD 69) — Tiberius Alexander first swearing the legions of Egypt to him on 1 July — and Vespasian, recalling the prophecy, freed Josephus.
results.answer:
The Jewish prisoner was **Josephus** (Flavius Josephus), the Jewish historian and former commander in Galilee. He made this prophecy to **Vespasian**, the Roman general sent to suppress the Jewish revolt.

According to Josephus's own account in *The Wars of the Jews*, when he was captured after the siege of Jotapata and brought before Vespasian, he declared: "Thou, O Vespasian, art Caesar and emperor, thou, and this thy son" [2]. Vespasian initially dismissed this as a cunning trick to save his life [2].

Suetonius corroborates the story, writing that "when Josephus, one of the noble prisoners, was put in chains, he confidently affirmed that he should be released in a very short time by the same Vespasian, but he would be emperor first" [8].

Cassius Dio records a similar version: "a Jew named Josephus, who had previously been disliked by him and imprisoned, gave a laugh and said: 'You may imprison me now, but a year later when you become emperor you will release me'" [22].

**The prophecy came true.** In 69 AD, during the chaotic "Year of the Four Emperors" following Nero's death, Vespasian was proclaimed emperor by his legions and was subsequently confirmed by the Senate. Josephus himself narrates the fulfillment: after becoming emperor, Vespasian "remember[ed] what Josephus had said to him when he ventured to foretell his coming to the empire while Nero was alive" and declared it shameful that the man who foretold his rise should remain a prisoner. He then ordered Josephus set free, and at Titus's suggestion, Josephus's chains were cut to pieces rather than merely unbound — a symbolic gesture making it "like a man that had never been bound at all" [1].

results.question: How did the reign of the emperor Vitellius come to its end?
results.ideal_answer: Vitellius's army was crushed by the Flavian forces at the second battle of Cremona (Bedriacum); when Rome fell, Vitellius was dragged from hiding through the streets to the Gemonian stairs, tortured and killed, and his body cast into the Tiber.
results.answer: Vitellius's reign ended in violent death during the Flavian capture of Rome. After Vespasian's forces under Antonius Primus fought their way into the city, Vitellius attempted to hide. According to Suetonius, he concealed himself in a porter's lodge (a dog-keeper's cell) but was dragged out, half-naked, with his hands bound behind his back and a rope around his neck, then paraded along the Via Sacra amid abuse and pelting with dung and mud before being tormented to death at the Scalae Gemoniae and dragged by a hook into the Tiber [1]. Cassius Dio similarly reports that Vitellius hid in an obscure alcove where dogs were kept, was discovered, stripped, bound, and dragged down the Sacred Way to the Forum, where he was humiliated and eventually struck down at the Scalae Gemoniae and beheaded; his head was then carried about the city. Dio adds that a Celtic soldier took pity on Vitellius during the procession, wounded him and killed himself, though Vitellius did not die from that wound [4]. Dio also notes that Vitellius's wife later saw to his burial, and that his brother Lucius, who had set out from Tarracina to assist him, was killed shortly after making terms, as was Vitellius's young son [2]. Tacitus's account largely concurs: Vitellius was driven along to the Gemoniae, forced to look upon his falling statues and the spot where Galba was slain, and fell under a shower of blows after answering a tribune's insults with "Yet I was your Emperor" [5]. Josephus gives a briefer summary, stating that after his forces were destroyed in battle, Vitellius came out of the palace drunk, was dragged through the multitude, abused with all sorts of torments, and had his head cut off in the midst of Rome [9]. Tacitus adds that the Capitoline had already been burned during the siege of Sabinus (Vespasian's brother), who was captured and killed by the Vitellianists before Vitellius's own fall [8][9].

results.question: Which oracle did Croesus famously test before his war with Persia, and what natural phenomenon there was later said to produce its prophecies?
results.ideal_answer: The oracle at Delphi, which Croesus tested (by the secret boiled-tortoise riddle) and judged the only true oracle before consulting it about war with Persia. Its prophecies were later attributed to an exhalation rising from a cleft in the earth, which inspired the Pythian priestess to her divine frenzy.
results.answer:
Croesus tested the Delphic oracle (the oracle of Apollo at Delphi) before his war with Persia, along with several others. He devised a test by boiling a tortoise and a lamb together in a bronze cauldron on a specific appointed day, and sent messengers to ask the oracles what he was doing at that moment. Only the Pythian priestess at Delphi (and possibly the oracle of Amphiaraus) answered correctly, describing the scene in hexameter verse and thereby convincing Croesus of the oracle's truthfulness [2].

The natural phenomenon later said to produce its prophecies was a vapour or exhalation rising from a chasm or cleft in the earth at the site. Diodorus Siculus reports that the oracle was discovered when goats feeding near an opening or gulf in the earth began to act strangely, and a shepherd who looked into the chasm was similarly inspired with a spirit of prophecy [6]. Pausanias similarly records that shepherds became inspired by the vapour (vapour) and prophesied through Apollo [7]. The phenomenon of an intoxicating vapour or exhalation from the cleft beneath the Delphic tripod is also referenced as a traditional explanation for the oracle's inspiration [10].

results.question: What naval catastrophe destroyed the great Athenian expedition to Sicily, and what became of its captured general?
results.ideal_answer: The Athenian armament was annihilated at Syracuse — beaten decisively in the Great Harbour and then destroyed "with a total destruction" in the retreat (413 BC). The captured general Nicias was put to death by the Syracusans (Thucydides), though Plutarch preserves a variant that he and Demosthenes were instead allowed to take their own lives.
results.answer:
The naval catastrophe that destroyed the great Athenian expedition to Sicily was the final sea‑battle in the **Great Harbour of Syracuse** in 413 BC. After the Syracusans and their allies sealed the mouth of the harbour with a barricade of ships, boats and chains, the Athenians made one desperate attempt to break out [13][11]. Fighting in a confined space that neutralised their usual seamanship, the Athenian fleet was totally defeated: ships were sunk, disabled, or captured, and the army was left stranded [15][13][5].

As for the captured generals, both **Nicias and Demosthenes were put to death**. Thucydides writes that they were *"butchered, against the will of Gylippus"*, who had hoped to carry them back to Sparta as trophies; the Syracusans and their Corinthian allies, however, feared Nicias might escape through his wealth or reveal secrets under torture [6]. Plutarch reports a variant tradition — that Hermocrates, unable to prevent the death sentence, secretly sent the generals the means to die by their own hands before the sentence was carried out [10]. Their bodies were then thrown out before the city gates for public spectacle [10][7].

results.question: Why is Roman concrete so durable compared to modern concrete?
results.answer:
The provided sources do not contain a direct comparison between Roman and modern concrete, nor a scientific explanation of why Roman concrete proves more durable. However, the ancient sources do describe the distinctive materials and properties that contributed to Roman concrete's longevity.

Strabo records that the sand around Puteoli (modern Pozzuoli) "contains much gypsum, and will cement and consolidate thoroughly," and notes that by "mixing this sand with chalk-stones they construct moles in the sea," creating structures that harden even underwater [6]. This is a clear description of the volcanic ash (pozzolana) that gave Roman mortar its hydraulic-setting capability.

Cassius Dio provides a complementary description of the volcanic earth at Baiae: a substance that, when exposed to dry air, crumbles to dust, but "when they are placed in a swirl of water and sand grow into a solid piece" and "becomes indissoluble" [2]. This captures the counter-intuitive property of Roman concrete—that it gained strength through contact with water, unlike ordinary lime mortar.

Mommsen, writing in the 19th century, still marvels at "the everlasting mortar of their buildings" as emblematic of "the indestructible solidity and the energetic vigour of the Roman character" [1].

These passages attest to the remarkable durability of Roman mortar and its unique volcanic ingredients, but a scientific explanation comparing it to modern Portland cement—or discussing modern research findings about its superior crack-resistance and self-healing properties—is not present in this corpus of mainly ancient and 19th-century historical texts.

results.question: How did the emperor Augustus react when he was assassinated in the Senate?
results.answer: Augustus was not assassinated in the Senate. According to the sources in the corpus, he died a natural death from illness at Nola on 19 August A.D. 14, at the age of seventy-five [2][14]. Cassius Dio reports that he fell sick and died, though some suspicion fell on Livia for possibly poisoning figs he ate, but this is presented as a rumour rather than established fact [3][2]. William Smith likewise records that his health declined and he died at Nola [14]. The nearest the sources come to an assassination is Gibbon's observation that Augustus, having seen the fate of Julius Caesar, carefully avoided provoking the Senate with monarchical titles and thus escaped Caesar's end [1]. There is no account of Augustus being killed in the Senate.

results.question: What were the symptoms of the epidemic that devastated Athens?
results.ideal_answer: Thucydides (who had the disease himself) describes the "distemper" thus: people in good health were suddenly attacked by violent heats in the head, redness and inflammation of the eyes, a bloody throat and tongue with fetid breath, then sneezing, hoarseness, chest pain and a hard cough, stomach upset and retching with violent spasms. Most died around the seventh to ninth day; in survivors the disease could descend into the bowels (fatal diarrhoea), or cost them fingers, toes, eyes, or memory.
results.answer:
According to Thucydides, who had the disease himself and carefully observed its course in others, the epidemic that devastated Athens presented the following symptoms [1][2]:

- **Sudden onset:** People in good health were all of a sudden attacked.
- **Head and eyes:** Violent heats in the head, with redness and inflammation in the eyes.
- **Throat and tongue:** The inward parts, such as the throat or tongue, became bloody and emitted an unnatural, fetid breath.
- **Respiratory symptoms:** Sneezing and hoarseness followed, after which the pain soon reached the chest and produced a hard cough.
- **Gastric distress:** When it settled in the stomach, it upset it, with discharges of every kind of bile named by physicians, accompanied by great distress. In most cases, ineffectual retching followed, producing violent spasms—sometimes ceasing soon after, sometimes persisting much longer.
- **Appearance of the skin:** Externally the body was not very hot to the touch nor pale in appearance, but reddish, livid, and breaking out into small pustules and ulcers.
- **Internal burning and thirst:** The patient burned internally so intensely that they could not bear clothing or linen even of the lightest description, preferring to be stark naked. They longed to throw themselves into cold water, and some neglected sick plunged into rain-tanks in their unquenchable thirst—though drinking little or much made no difference.
- **Sleeplessness:** The miserable feeling of not being able to rest or sleep never ceased to torment them.
- **Course of the disease:** The body did not waste away while the distemper was at its height, holding out to a marvel. Most succumbed on the seventh or eighth day to the internal inflammation, still retaining some strength. If they passed this stage and the disease descended further into the bowels, inducing violent ulceration with severe diarrhoea, this brought on a weakness that was generally fatal.
- **Lasting effects on survivors:** Even where it did not prove mortal, it left its mark on the extremities—settling in the privy parts, the fingers, and the toes, causing many to escape with the loss of these parts, and some with the loss of their eyes. Others were seized with an entire loss of memory on first recovery, knowing neither themselves nor their friends.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every symptom and detail in the answer is directly supported by the cited Thucydides passages, with no invented or unsupported content. | completeness: The answer fully covers every symptom category the question asks for, matching the reference's coverage of sudden onset, head and eye inflammation, throat and respiratory issues, gastric distress, disease progression, and lasting effects on survivors. | attribution: The answer correctly attributes the detailed symptoms to Thucydides in prose, and since the provided sources do not disagree on this point, no further attribution of conflicting views is required.

results.question: What role did mass kidnapping play in early Rome's search for wives?
results.ideal_answer: Rome under Romulus was short of women and neighboring peoples refused intermarriage, so Romulus staged games (the Consualia) and invited the neighbors; at a signal the Roman youths rushed in and carried off the unmarried women — the "rape of the Sabine women." The ensuing war with the Sabines ended with the women themselves brokering peace and the two peoples merging.
results.answer: Mass kidnapping played a central role in early Rome's search for wives. After Romulus founded the city, Rome's population consisted largely of men, including fugitives and outcasts, but the neighboring communities refused to intermarry with them [5][2]. In response, Romulus staged a festival—the Consualia, in honor of Neptune Equestris—and invited the surrounding peoples, especially the Sabines, to attend [2][3]. At a prearranged signal, the Roman youth rushed upon the guests and carried off the unmarried women by force [2][3]. The number of women seized is variously reported: Plutarch notes that some sources say only thirty were taken, while others give figures of 527 or 683 [3]. The women were distributed among Roman men, with the most beautiful reserved for leading senators [2]. This mass abduction was explicitly a strategy to secure wives and thereby ensure the city's future through alliances and offspring [3][5]. The event led to war with the Sabines and other neighbors, but the kidnapped women eventually intervened to reconcile the two sides, resulting in the union of Romans and Sabines [4].
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer is mostly grounded in the provided passages, but it embellishes the outcome by asserting that the women’s intervention resulted in the “union of Romans and Sabines,” a conclusion not found in any of the source texts shown. | completeness: The answer fully explains the central role, context, execution, and consequences of the mass kidnapping in securing wives for early Rome, covering everything the question asks. | attribution: The answer accurately synthesizes the complementary sources and correctly attributes the differing historical estimates of the number of abducted women to Plutarch in prose, with no misattributions.

results.question: How did the Sabine commander Mettius Curtius end up in a swamp during the battle for Rome?
results.ideal_answer: During the battle between Romans and Sabines on the future site of the Forum, Mettius Curtius' horse took fright at the noise of his pursuers and carried him headlong into a morass; he barely escaped (Plutarch says he abandoned the mired horse and saved himself). The spot was afterwards called the Curtian Lake.
results.answer:
According to the ancient sources, the Sabine commander Mettius Curtius (or Mettus Curtius in Livy's spelling) ended up in a swamp during the battle for Rome in slightly differing accounts.

Plutarch relates that the river had overflowed a few days before the battle, leaving a deep blind mud and slime in the plain where the Forum now stands — a deceitful and dangerous surface that was not easily spotted. Curtius, a gallant man eager for honor, was mounted on horseback and galloping ahead of the rest when he became mired in this bog. Despite whipping, spurring, and shouting, he could not free his horse, so he abandoned it and saved himself. The place, Plutarch notes, was thereafter called the Curtian Lake [1].

Livy provides a different version: during the battle, Mettus Curtius was fighting on horseback and was repulsed by Romulus and his band of young warriors. As the Romans pursued him, his horse took fright at the noise of the pursuers and threw itself — and Curtius — into a lake. This diverted the attention of the Sabines, who were anxious over the risk to so important a man. Encouraged by his comrades beckoning and calling to him, Curtius eventually escaped from the water. The battle then resumed in the valley between the hills [7].

In summary, both authors agree that Curtius ended up in a body of water during the battle (a swampy lake left by the river), but they differ on the circumstances: Plutarch attributes it to Curtius's own headlong advance into hidden mire, while Livy says his horse bolted in fright from the pursuing Romans and plunged into the lake.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: The answer invents a detailed Livyan account in which Mettus Curtius's horse bolts into a lake and he is rescued by comrades, a narrative found in none of the provided passages and directly contradicted by Livy passage [5], which states the Curtian Lake was named after Marcus Curtius, not Curtius Mettus. | completeness: The answer comprehensively covers how Curtius ended up in the swamp by presenting both Plutarch's and Livy's accounts, including the frightened horse, the hidden mire, his escape, and the resulting Curtian Lake, leaving nothing the question asks for unaddressed. | attribution: The answer misattributes the story of Curtius's horse taking fright and plunging into a lake to Livy [7], but the provided text for that passage does not contain this information.

results.question: What omens were believed to have foretold Claudius' death?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius lists as the chief presages a comet, lightning striking his father Drusus' monument, and the deaths of magistrates of all ranks that year. Cassius Dio adds a shower of blood, a thunderbolt on the Praetorian standards, the temple of Jupiter Victor opening of its own accord, and bees swarming in the camp.
results.answer:
Several omens were believed to have foretold the death of the emperor Claudius, as recorded by Cassius Dio, Suetonius, and Tacitus.

Cassius Dio lists the following portents: a comet star seen over a wide area, a shower of blood, a lightning bolt that struck the standards of the Praetorian Guard, the spontaneous opening of the temple of Jupiter Victor, a swarm of bees in the military camp, and the death of one holder of each political office [2].

Suetonius reports that the chief presages were the appearance of a comet, his father Drusus's monument being struck by lightning, and the death of most magistrates of all ranks that year [6]. He also notes that Claudius himself seemed aware of his approaching end, making ominous remarks in his final public appearances [6].

Tacitus, writing about the year of the consulship of Marcus Asinius and Manius Acilius (AD 54, the year Claudius died), records a series of prodigies portending political change for the worse: lightning set soldiers' standards and tents ablaze, a swarm of bees settled on the summit of the Capitol, monstrous births (half-man, half-beast, and a pig with hawk's talons) were reported, and the reduction in the number of magistrates — a quaestor, an aedile, a tribune, a praetor, and a consul all dying within a few months — was itself considered a portent [9].

Thus the sources broadly agree on a comet appearing, lightning striking important objects (the Praetorian standards in Dio, Drusus's monument in Suetonius, the soldiers' standards and tents in Tacitus), a swarm of bees in a significant location, and the death of multiple magistrates as foretelling Claudius' demise.
results.judge_notes: faithfulness: Every claim about the omens is directly supported by the cited source passages, with no invented details. | completeness: The answer fully addresses the question by comprehensively listing the omens from Suetonius and Cassius Dio found in the reference, along with additional corroborating details from Tacitus, without omitting anything required. | attribution: The sources provide complementary lists of omens without making incompatible claims, and the answer correctly attributes the specific details from each author in clear prose.

results.question: Does Thucydides describe anything like a tsunami?
results.ideal_answer: Yes. He reports that during the earthquakes of 426 BC the sea at Orobiae in Euboea retired from the coast and returned in a huge wave, flooding part of the town and leaving some of it permanently under water, killing those who could not reach high ground (with a similar inundation at Atalanta). He even gives the correct causal explanation: the earthquake drives the sea back, and it recoils with redoubled force.
results.answer: Yes, Thucydides describes a tsunami-like event. He recounts that during the sixth year of the Peloponnesian War, after numerous earthquakes, the sea at Orobiae in Euboea retreated from the coast and then returned in a huge wave, flooding much of the town and leaving part of it permanently under water. A similar inundation occurred at Atalanta, carrying away part of an Athenian fort and wrecking a ship. He explicitly attributes the phenomenon to the earthquake, explaining that the sea is driven back by the shock and then recoils with redoubled force, causing the inundation [1].

results.question: How were the pontoon bridges across the Hellespont built?
results.ideal_answer: After a storm destroyed the first pair (built by Phoenicians with flax cables and Egyptians with papyrus cables), the rebuilt bridges rested on rows of anchored fifty-oared galleys and triremes — 360 under one bridge and 314 under the other — over which cables of white flax and papyrus were stretched tight with wooden windlasses; planks, brushwood, and stamped earth were laid on top, with barriers so the animals would not see the sea.
results.answer:
The pontoon bridges across the Hellespont, built for Xerxes' invasion of Greece, consisted of two separate bridges of boats stretching from near Abydos to the European shore between Sestos and Madytus (a distance of about seven furlongs) [5][2]. 

The first attempt, constructed by Phoenician and Egyptian engineers, used cables of white flax (Phoenician) and papyrus (Egyptian) but was destroyed by a storm, after which Xerxes beheaded the engineers [5][2]. Reconstruction was entrusted to new chief-constructors, who proceeded as follows [5]:

1. **Ships**: They moored 360 fifty-oared galleys and triremes under the bridge nearer the Euxine (Black Sea) and 314 under the other bridge, the vessels lying in the direction of the Hellespont stream to support rope tension [5][2].
2. **Anchors**: Very large anchors were let down on both sides — towards the Pontus to resist winds from within, and towards the Aegean to resist south-east and south winds [5].
3. **Openings**: Gaps were left between the ships and under the cables to allow small trading vessels to pass through to and from the Pontus (three such gaps according to Bury) [5][2].
4. **Cables**: Six huge cables were stretched across each line of ships — two of white flax and four of papyrus — strained tight with wooden windlasses. The flaxen ropes were heavier; a cubit weighed one talent [5][2].
5. **Decking**: Logs of wood were sawn to the breadth of the bridge, laid across the stretched ropes, and fastened above. A second layer of cables may have been laid above the planks to hold them in place [6][2].
6. **Road surface**: Brushwood was laid over the logs, then earth was carried on and stamped down firmly to form a road [6][2].
7. **Palisades**: Barriers (palisades) were built along each side, high enough to prevent baggage-animals and horses from seeing the water and becoming frightened [6][2].

Xerxes subsequently crossed from a marble throne erected on the shore, with the troops crossing under the lash, the passage taking two days [2].

results.question: How did the Persian royal postal relay system work?
results.ideal_answer: According to Herodotus, men and horses were stationed along the route, one for each day's journey; the message was carried by the first rider to the second, the second to the third, and so on, like the Greek torch-race. Neither snow, rain, heat, nor darkness of night stopped them — nothing mortal travelled faster. The Persians called it the angareion.
results.answer: The Persian royal postal relay system, called the **angareion** (ἀγγαρήιον), was a mounted courier service that used a chain of men and horses stationed at regular intervals along the royal roads. Herodotus describes it as follows: for the entire length of a journey a horse and a rider were posted for each day's travel; a courier would ride the first stage at top speed and hand the dispatch to the next courier, who would ride the next stage on a fresh horse, and so on until the message reached its destination. Neither snow, rain, heat, nor darkness of night could stop a messenger from completing his assigned stage as fast as possible [1]. Herodotus likened the hand‑off to a torch‑race run in honour of Hephaistos [1]. George Rawlinson, summarising classical accounts, adds that at each post‑house the state maintained multiple couriers and relays of horses; the messengers travelled by night as well as by day, and the speed of the service was so great that some compared it to the flight of birds. The routes were furnished with inns, bridges or ferries, guard‑houses, and were kept secure from brigands [2].

results.answer: The celibate Jewish brotherhood that held all property in common and despised private wealth is the Essenes, described at length by Josephus. results.question: What surprise attack on the beached Athenian fleet brought the long war between Athens and Sparta to an end?
results.ideal_answer: The battle of Aegospotami (405 BC). With the Athenian crews scattered ashore in search of food, Lysander rowed across the Hellespont and caught the fleet drawn up on the open beach; all but a handful of ships (Conon escaped with nine) were taken without a fight. The loss of the fleet left Athens unable to continue the Peloponnesian War, and she surrendered the next year.
results.answer: The surprise attack on the beached Athenian fleet that brought the long war between Athens and Sparta — the Peloponnesian War — to an end was the **Battle of Aegospotami** (405 BC). After days of the Athenians carelessly beaching their ships and dispersing ashore for meals, the Spartan commander Lysander launched a sudden assault across the Hellespont. Xenophon describes how, on the fifth day, Lysander's scout ships hoisted a bright shield as a signal the moment the Athenian crews had disembarked; then the entire Peloponnesian fleet rowed swiftly across the strait and caught the Athenian triremes at their moorings, most of them deserted or only partially manned [4]. George Grote likewise recounts that "nothing could be more complete or decisive than the surprise of the Athenian fleet," with nearly all 180 ships captured on the beach [1]. J.B. Bury notes that "there was no battle, no resistance" — 160 ships were taken at once [2]. Plutarch, in his Life of Lysander, relates how the attack was over in a single hour and explicitly states that it "finished... a war which had been protracted in its continuance, and diversified in its incidents and its fortunes to a degree exceeding belief... it was now put an end to by the good counsel and ready conduct of one man" [7].

results.question: How were the victorious Athenian commanders punished for failing to rescue survivors after the sea battle at Arginusae?
results.ideal_answer: After winning at Arginusae (406 BC) the generals were accused of failing to pick up the shipwrecked crews (they blamed a storm). The Athenian assembly, illegally trying them collectively rather than one by one, condemned the eight; the six who had returned to Athens were executed. The city soon repented of the act.
results.answer: The eight Athenian generals present at Arginusae were tried by the Assembly rather than the regular courts and were condemned to death and confiscation of property in a single collective vote, violating the customary requirement of separate trials [1]. Six of the generals—including Thrasyllus and Pericles (son of the great statesman)—were executed [1][3]. The remaining two, Protomachus and Aristogenes, evaded punishment by declining to return to Athens and choosing voluntary exile [1][4].

results.question: How was a Roman army's overnight fortified camp laid out, with its regular streets and gates?
results.ideal_answer: On a fixed, repeatable plan. The general's tent (praetorium) was set first, then the lines of the tribunes' and legions' quarters measured off from it; the whole camp formed a square crossed by regular streets (viae) "like a town", with four gates, a market-place, and a broad clear space inside the rampart — so familiar that each soldier could find his quarters as in his own city.
results.answer:
Polybius, in Book VI of his *Histories*, gives the most detailed surviving description of a Roman army's overnight fortified camp (castra). According to his account, the camp was laid out as a regular square — or an oblong when two consular armies camped together — with streets and other constructions "regularly planned like a town" [2].

**The Central Area (Praetorium).** The camp layout began by selecting the best spot for the general's tent, the Praetorium. A square of 200 feet per side was measured around this point [7]. On one side of the Praetorium (whichever offered the best access to water and forage) lay the market-place (forum) and the Quaestorium (the quartermaster's office and stores) [4].

**Streets (Viae).** The camp was divided by a grid of streets. A 100-foot-wide road ran from side to side behind the line of tribunes' tents, skirting the market-place, Praetorium, and Quaestorium [2]. Five parallel viae ran lengthwise through the quarters of the legions and allies, separating the blocks of cavalry and infantry maniples [4]. The *Via Quintana*, a 50-foot-wide cross-street at right angles to these, ran parallel to the tribunes' tents at the level of the fifth squadrons and maniples, bisecting the rear portion of the camp [4].

**Gates.** There were four gates, each at the end of a main thoroughfare: the *Porta Principalis Dextra* (on the right side) and *Porta Principalis Sinistra* (on the left side), both at the ends of the 100-foot road in front of the Praetorium; the *Porta Decumana* at the rear, opposite the Praetorium [1][5]; and the front gate (implicitly the Porta Praetoria) on the side toward which the legions faced. Between the tents and the rampart (agger) on every side, an open space of 200 feet was left for the movement of troops, cattle, and booty, and to keep enemy missiles out of range of the tents [2][9].

**Soldiers' Quarters.** The quarters were arranged symmetrically on either side of the central axis. The twelve tribunes' tents formed a straight line 50 feet from the Praetorium. The cavalry and infantry of the two legions, along with the allied contingents and the picked extraordinarii, were quartered in rectangular blocks of fixed dimensions, each maniple occupying a square of 100 feet frontage. The entire arrangement was so standardized that a soldier, seeing the layout marked out by coloured flags and spears, "knows precisely on which via, and at what point of it, his quarters are to be, because all occupy the same position in the camp wherever it may be — it is exactly like a legion entering its own city" [6].

results.question: What strategy of refusing open battle and wearing the invader down by delay was used against Hannibal in Italy?
results.ideal_answer: The cautious, delaying strategy of the dictator Quintus Fabius Maximus, who shadowed Hannibal from the heights, cut off his supplies and refused a pitched battle — earning the surname Cunctator ("the Delayer"/"Lingerer") and, at first, scorn at Rome, before it was recognised as the policy that saved the state.
results.answer: The strategy was the delaying (or "Fabian") strategy of Quintus Fabius Maximus, who was appointed dictator after the Roman defeats at Lake Trasimene. Rather than risk a pitched battle against Hannibal's veteran army, Fabius kept his troops on high ground, shadowed the enemy at a moderate distance, denied them opportunities to forage or plunder, and refused every challenge to fight a set engagement [4][1][3]. His object was twofold: to gradually whittle down Hannibal's limited forces through small successes while his own army recovered its confidence, and to exploit Rome's inexhaustible supply of provisions and men [4]. Livy notes that even when his own camp and Rome itself condemned his inaction, Fabius 'inflexibly adhered to the same line of policy' through the remainder of the summer [5]. Cassius Dio records that Hannibal grew so perplexed by Fabius's refusal to fight that he briefly started for Rome before turning back [2].

results.question: How did Hannibal's men break apart the rock blocking a pass while crossing the Alps?
results.ideal_answer: They split it by fire and vinegar: piling and burning timber against the rock to heat it, then pouring vinegar on the heated stone to make it soft and crumbling, after which they cut a way through with iron tools.
results.answer: According to Livy, when Hannibal's army was blocked by a cliff, the soldiers felled large trees to build a huge pile of timber. They waited for a strong wind and set the pile on fire. While the rocks were heated, they poured vinegar (sour wine) on them, which rendered the stones soft and crumbling. They then broke apart the rock with iron tools and cut a winding path down the declivity, allowing not only the pack animals but also the elephants to descend. [1]

results.ideal_answer: The Bacchanalia — the secret night-rites of Bacchus. After they spread from Etruria through Italy and into Rome, the senate in 186 BC treated them as a dangerous conspiracy: thousands were tried and many executed, the meeting places demolished, and a decree forbade the rites in Rome or Italy except under strict, tiny, senate-licensed exceptions.

results.question: What Greek coastal city was swallowed by the sea after an earthquake, leaving its ruins visible beneath the water?
results.ideal_answer: Helike, in Achaea. One winter night (373 BC) a violent earthquake struck and the sea simultaneously rushed in, submerging the city and its people; for a long time afterwards its ruins could be seen under the water, growing fainter as the sea wore them away.

According to Pausanias, an earthquake struck the city, and simultaneously the sea encroached over the land, flooding Helice and submerging it entirely — even the grove of Poseidon was covered so that only the treetops were visible [1]. He notes that the ruins of Helice were still visible beneath the water in his time, though less clearly than before due to the action of the sea [1]. Diodorus Siculus likewise records that Helice and Bura were the two cities of Achaia that chiefly suffered when the sea raged and swallowed up both houses and inhabitants after an earthquake [2]. Strabo adds that the inundation occurred during the night, that the city had stood twelve stadia from the sea, and that the disaster happened two years before the battle of Leuctra [5].

results.question: How did the soldiers turn against the emperor Galba and kill him in the forum?
results.ideal_answer: Bought over by Otho's promises, the praetorians abandoned Galba; as he was carried into the Forum his bearers panicked and tipped him from his litter near the Lacus Curtius, where the soldiers cut him down — most reports saying a legionary named Camurius severed his throat — and then beheaded and mutilated the old emperor, consul and high priest, before the senators and crowd.
results.answer:
The soldiers turned against the emperor Galba after being won over by Marcus Salvius Otho, who was angry at not being adopted as Galba's heir. Otho conspired with a few soldiers and then bought the loyalty of the rest with promises [1]. When Galba learned of the unrest, he was lured out of the palace by false reports that the mutiny had been quelled and that Otho had been killed [2][5]. A soldier even approached him with a bloody sword, claiming to have slain Otho; Galba asked, "Who gave you your orders?" [1][2][5].

Reassured, Galba set out for the Capitol to offer sacrifice, but as he reached the Roman Forum, the situation reversed: news arrived that Otho had in fact seized the praetorian camp [5]. Horsemen and foot-soldiers then met him in the Forum. According to Cassius Dio, they "cut down in the presence of many senators and crowds of plebeians the old man, their consul, high priest, Caesar, emperor" [1]. Suetonius reports that the knights appointed to dispatch him galloped up and put him to death after his attendants abandoned him [2]. Tacitus provides the most vivid detail: the soldiers burst into the Forum at full speed, trampling the crowd and the Senate; the standard-bearer of Galba's escort tore down his effigy, signaling the troops' defection to Otho [3]. Near the lake of Curtius, Galba was thrown from his litter and fell to the ground. His last words are variously reported—some say he begged for a few days to pay a donative, others that he offered his neck and bade them strike for the good of the Commonwealth [4]. The actual killer is uncertain, but the current report in Tacitus names Camurius, a soldier of the 15th legion, who severed his throat by treading his sword down upon it. The soldiers then mutilated his body, cut off his head, and stuck it on a pole [1][4].

results.question: On what furniture did well-to-do Romans recline while eating the main meal of the day?
results.ideal_answer: They reclined on couches (lecti) rather than sitting. The cena (dinner) was the chief meal of the day, and after the Punic wars special dining rooms — the triclinia, named for the three couches arranged around the table — were built for the comfort of reclining guests, with no more than three to a couch as good manners.
results.answer: Well-to-do Romans reclined on **dining couches** (Latin *lecti* or *lecti tricliniares*) while eating the main meal of the day. In the special dining-room called the *triclinium*, three such couches were arranged around a central table (*mensa*): the *lectus medius*, *lectus summus*, and *lectus imus*, each accommodating three diners for a total of nine [2][1]. The couches were comfortably draped, and the arrangement was designed for ease, conversation, and the supervision of the meal [2][3].
