results.question: How does Plutarch portray Antony's ruin through Cleopatra?
results.ideal_answer: Plutarch frames Cleopatra as "the last and crowning mischief" that befell Antony — a love that awakened his dormant passions and corrupted what goodness and judgment remained. She kept him captive through ceaseless charm (dicing, drinking, hunting with him, never letting him escape by day or night); the passion rekindled "like Plato's restive horse" when he returned east and showered her with provinces; and after Actium it left him a misanthrope playing Timon of Athens in a sea-hut, before the final catastrophe in Alexandria.
results.answer:
Plutarch portrays Antony's ruin through Cleopatra as a gradual moral and political collapse driven by an overwhelming, irrational passion that corrupts his judgment, alienates his Roman allies, and ultimately destroys him both as a commander and as a man.

**Cleopatra as the "crowning mischief"**

Plutarch introduces Cleopatra as the culminating disaster of Antony's life. He writes that "the last and crowning mischief that could befall him came in the love of Cleopatra, to awaken and kindle to fury passions that as yet lay still and dormant in his nature, and to stifle and finally corrupt any elements that yet made resistance in him, of goodness and a sound judgment" [1]. The language is that of a moral disease: Cleopatra does not create new vices but awakens latent ones and extinguishes whatever virtue remained.

**Enslavement of the will**

Plutarch repeatedly emphasizes that Antony lost control of his own will. After a period of separation, his passion "gathered strength again, and broke out into a flame. And, in fine, like Plato's restive and rebellious horse of the human soul, flinging off all good and wholesome counsel, and breaking fairly loose" [5]. The Platonic image of the soul's unruly horse vividly casts reason as overthrown by appetite. In the formal comparison with Demetrius, Plutarch sharpens the point: "Antony, like Hercules in the picture where Omphale is seen removing his club and stripping him of his lion's skin, was over and over again disarmed by Cleopatra, and beguiled away, while great actions and enterprises of the first necessity fell, as it were, from his hands, to go with her to the seashore of Canopus and Taphosiris, and play about" [13]. The Hercules-Omphale image is one of emasculation: the great hero stripped of his strength by a woman.

**Public humiliation and alienation of Rome**

Plutarch shows Antony's infatuation leading to public acts of subservience that scandalized Roman sensibilities. He reports that Antony gave Cleopatra vast territories—Phoenicia, Coele-Syria, Cyprus, parts of Cilicia, Judaea, and Arabia—"profuse gifts, which much displeased the Romans" and that "nothing stung the Romans like the shame of these honors paid to Cleopatra" [5]. He further records that Antony acknowledged his twin children by her, naming them Alexander and Cleopatra with the titles Sun and Moon [5]. Calvisius charged that Antony had "risen up and rubbed her feet" at a banquet, allowed the Ephesians to salute her as queen, and would leave the tribunal mid-case to follow her chair [4]. His friends who objected were driven away by Cleopatra's flatterers [4]. Caesar formally declared war on Cleopatra, not Antony, and said Antony "had drunk potions that had bereaved him of his senses" and that the real generals were "Mardion the eunuch, Pothinus, Iras, Cleopatra's hairdressing girl, and Charmion" [7].

**The catastrophe at Actium**

The Battle of Actium is the dramatic turning point where Cleopatra's influence proves fatal. When her sixty ships suddenly hoisted sail and fled, Antony "showed to all the world that he was no longer actuated by the thoughts and motives of a commander or a man, or indeed by his own judgment at all... as if he had been born part of her, and must move with her wheresoever she went, as soon as he saw her ship sailing away, he abandoned all that were fighting and spending their lives for him" and followed her—"her that had so well begun his ruin and would hereafter accomplish it" [2]. In the comparison, Plutarch drives the point home: "like another Paris, he left the battle to fly to her arms; or rather, to say the truth, Paris fled when he was already beaten; Antony fled first, and, to follow Cleopatra, abandoned his victory" [13].

**The final betrayal and death**

After the defeat, Antony cried out "that Cleopatra had betrayed him to the enemies he had made for her sake" [3]. Cleopatra, fearing his fury, shut herself in her monument and sent word she was dead. Antony, believing it, declared that "Fate has snatched away the only pretext for which you could say you desired yet to live" and stabbed himself [3]. He was then drawn up, dying, into her monument, where he expired in her arms [9]. Plutarch notes that after his death, "his statues were all thrown down, but those of Cleopatra were left untouched" [6]—a final ironic detail underscoring that Antony lost everything, while Cleopatra's image survived through bribery.

**The comparison's verdict**

In the formal comparison with Demetrius, Plutarch delivers his summary judgment: "Antony procured his ruin by his marriage" to Cleopatra, a "foreign and unlawful woman" [11]. The contrast is explicit: Demetrius suffered no harm from his multiple marriages, but Antony's union with Cleopatra was the direct cause of his destruction [11].

results.question: How does Arrian portray Alexander's bond with his soldiers?
results.ideal_answer: As intense mutual devotion that survived even open conflict. At the Hyphasis the army's silent, grieved resistance forced the invincible king to turn back; when he nearly died among the Malli, the troops wept, cheered, and crowded to touch his hands, knees, and clothes as he showed himself alive; and at Opis the mutiny dissolved when he called every Macedonian his kinsman, sealing the reconciliation with kisses and a vast common banquet.
results.answer:
Arrian portrays Alexander's bond with his soldiers as a complex, deeply emotional, and reciprocal relationship — one grounded in shared hardship, mutual devotion, moments of profound tension, and ultimate reconciliation.

**Shared danger and the leader's example.** Alexander repeatedly reminds his men that he has never asked them to endure what he himself has not suffered. In his speech at Opis, he challenges them: "Who is there of you who knows that he has endured greater toil for me than I have for him? Come now! whoever of you has wounds, let him strip and show them, and I will show mine in turn; for there is no part of my body, in front at any rate, remaining free from wounds" [3]. He recites every weapon that has marked him — sword, arrow, engine-missile, stone, and bolt — and declares he was struck "for the sake of your lives, your glory, and your wealth, I am still leading you as conquerors" [3]. Before Gaugamela, he likewise inspires the army by praising individual soldiers by name for their past feats and by modestly recalling his own daring in battle [2].

**Personal care for the rank-and-file.** After the battle of Issus, despite suffering his own thigh wound, Alexander "visited the wounded" and "collected the bodies of the slain" for a splendid burial; he then spoke "with eulogy to those whom he himself had recognised performing any gallant deed" and rewarded them with money according to their deserts [9]. In the Opis speech he underscores his specific benefactions: he celebrated the soldiers' weddings alongside his own, made their children akin to his, liquidated their debts without inquiry, and ensured that the fallen received glorious funerals and brass statues at home while their parents were exempted from public burdens [3].

**Soldiers' anxious devotion.** Arrian depicts the army's bond from the soldiers' side as visceral. At the Mallian citadel, when Alexander leaps inside alone, the Macedonians break the ladders in their frenzy to join him; discovering him gravely wounded, "they all raised a loud lamentation and howl of grief" and fought desperately around his body [7]. When he recovers and sails to camp, the men are incredulous and then weep at the sight of him alive, some touching his hands, knees, or clothing, others throwing garlands and flowers, while the whole army resounds with applause [10]. Nearchus records that some friends reproached Alexander for exposing himself to danger "in the front of the army in battle; which they said was the duty of a private soldier, and not that of the general" — a rebuke, Arrian notes, that angered Alexander precisely because he knew it was correct [10].

**Strains and limits.** The bond is not free of conflict. On the Hyphasis, worn out by endless toils, the Macedonians refuse to advance farther, and conferences in the camp reveal a hardening resolve "that they would not follow Alexander any farther, even if he should lead the way" [16]. Alexander summons the officers and tries persuasion, but Coenus's appeal on behalf of the soldiers moves the audience to tears, and Alexander, angry but unable to change their minds, withdraws to his tent. When even the sacrifice-victims prove unfavorable, he "made known to the army that he had resolved to march back again" [18]. Arrian shows a leader who is forced to yield, yet whose anger shows how much he relied on their commitment.

At Opis, Alexander's announcement of discharging the unfit backfires: the Macedonians feel despised, and their pent-up resentment over the Persian dress and the integration of foreign troops erupts into open defiance. Alexander, now "more hasty in temper than heretofore," has thirteen ringleaders seized and executed on the spot [11]. Yet after his speech recounting his wounds and his labors for their sake, the men cannot bear the separation: they gather outside his tent lamenting and refuse to leave [14].

**Reconciliation and kinship.** The climax of Arrian's portrayal is the reconciliation. When a veteran cavalry captain, Callines, says the Macedonians are grieved because Persians have been made Alexander's kinsmen while they have not, Alexander interrupts: "All of you without exception I consider my kinsmen, and so from this time I shall call you." Callines salutes him with a kiss, and all follow [14]. Alexander then presides over a public banquet of 9,000 — Macedonians, Persians, and men of other nations seated together — and prays "that harmony and community of rule might exist between the Macedonians and Persians" [14]. For Arrian, the bond between Alexander and his soldiers is thus a fusion of shared suffering, passionate loyalty, sharp conflict, and finally a renewed compact sealed in the language of family.

results.question: How does Livy portray the struggle between patricians and plebeians?
results.ideal_answer: As the defining, recurring affliction of the early Republic — "the discord among the several orders... the bane of this city." Driven by debt and oppression, the commons twice seceded (the first ended by Menenius Agrippa's fable of the belly and the members, the second on the Sacred Mount against the decemvirs); each crisis extracted new concessions — tribunes, appeal, laws — while Livy moralizes on how hard moderation is when each side's pursuit of power or liberty makes it an object of dread to the other.
results.answer:
Livy portrays the struggle between patricians and plebeians as a protracted conflict driven by patrician intransigence and plebeian desire for liberty and rights, culminating in a strengthened republic through negotiated compromise. He presents the patricians as reluctant to concede any power: when the Valerio-Horatian laws secured plebeian gains, the patricians felt that “whatever provision was made for securing the liberty of the commons … was a diminution made in their own power” [6]. Their resistance is epitomised by Appius Claudius, who denounced the tribunes as seditious and argued that the plebeian soldiers had abandoned their country through secession [c7189][11]. A patrician speaker later laments the endless dissensions, complaining that while the Volscians attacked, the plebeians turned their arms against the patricians rather than the enemy [7].

In contrast, Livy often emphasises the moderation and justice of the plebeian cause. During the secession to the Sacred Mount, the plebeians were “imitating the moderation of their fathers by committing no violence” [12]. When presenting their demands for the restoration of tribunes and the right of appeal, it was evident that “more hope was placed in the justice of their case than in arms” [8]. The story of the decemvirate—where Appius Claudius’s tyranny, exemplified by the attempted enslavement of Verginia, provoked a second secession—shows the plebeians overthrowing an oppressive magistracy and re-establishing the tribuneship, leading to “the restoration of peace and concord to the state” [13][c7182][c7203].

Livy also recounts the later Licinio-Sextian struggle, where the tribunes proposed laws concerning debt, land, and the opening of the consulship to plebeians, framing it as a contest for “land, money, and honours” that terrified the patricians [9]. Throughout, he acknowledges that the conflict could weaken Rome (external enemies exploited the discord [7]), but his narrative consistently moves toward resolution through concession, culminating in the gradual equalisation of the orders. Overall, Livy’s portrayal is sympathetic to the plebeians, depicting the struggle as a necessary and largely just process that ultimately produced a more cohesive and powerful republic.

results.question: How did the Romans become a naval power during the First Punic War, according to Polybius?
results.ideal_answer: Through what Polybius calls extraordinary audacity. Having never thought of naval war and lacking even the knowledge to build quinqueremes, they used a wrecked Carthaginian ship as their model, trained rowers on benches set up on dry land, and invented the "crow" boarding-bridge that turned sea battles into land fights (first proven at Mylae); after catastrophic storm losses they persisted, rebuilt with lighter ships and trained crews, and won the decisive battle of Aegusa.
results.answer:
According to Polybius, the Romans turned to the sea because the First Punic War was dragging on inconclusively — while Rome was superior on land, Carthage's navy dominated the coasts and kept Libya safe from invasion [1]. Seeing that the balance of success kept shifting, they resolved to build a fleet of 100 quinqueremes and 20 triremes [1].

However, their shipbuilders had no experience constructing quinqueremes, since no one in Italy used such vessels at the time [1]. Polybius notes that they had earlier lacked even a single decked warship, borrowing galleys from Greek cities in southern Italy merely to ferry troops to Messina [1]. The breakthrough came purely by chance: during that earlier crossing, a decked Carthaginian vessel charged so furiously in the strait that it ran aground and was captured. This ship served the Romans as a model on which they built their entire fleet, and Polybius states explicitly that without it they would have been wholly unable to carry out their design for want of constructive knowledge [2].

While the ships were under construction, crews were recruited and taught to row on dry land. The Romans seated the men on rowers' benches arranged in the same order as in an actual vessel, stationed a 'Celeustes' (the time-keeper) in their midst, and drilled them to pull and recover their oars in unison at his word [2]. Once the ships were built and launched, the crews had only a brief practice at real sea-rowing before sailing along the Italian coast to begin operations [2]. Polybius presents this entire undertaking — building a fleet from scratch with no prior naval experience and immediately challenging the dominant sea power — as the most striking proof of Roman audacity [1][2].

results.question: How does Thucydides describe the moral collapse caused by civil war?
results.ideal_answer: In his analysis of the Corcyra revolution, Thucydides argues that war is "a rough master" that levels men's characters to their fortunes. Words lost their ordinary meanings (recklessness became courage, moderation cowardice); religion was honored by neither side while fair phrases served guilty ends; the moderates perished between the factions; the "ancient simplicity" of honor was laughed out of existence and no man trusted his fellow. The concrete horror at Corcyra — fathers killing sons, suppliants slain at altars — set the pattern the whole Hellenic world then exceeded.
results.answer:
Thucydides describes the moral collapse caused by civil war as a complete inversion of values and language. Words lost their ordinary meaning and were twisted to fit new, debased standards: reckless audacity was called courage, prudent hesitation was called cowardice, moderation was a cloak for unmanliness, and ability to see all sides of a question was deemed inaptness to act [7]. Frantic violence became the mark of manliness; cautious plotting was praised as self-defence [7]. The most extreme advocates were deemed most trustworthy, while their opponents were suspected; plotting and counter-plotting were both commended, so that even blood ties grew weaker than party allegiance [7]. These party associations rested not on religious sanction but on complicity in crime; oaths of reconciliation were sworn only for immediate convenience and broken as soon as an opportunity for revenge arose, with treacherous vengeance thought sweeter than open conflict [7].

The root cause, he says, was the lust for power born of greed and ambition [7][c1216]. Leaders on both sides—one claiming political equality for the people, the other a moderate aristocracy—pursued private gain while pretending to serve the public good, and in their struggles they stopped at no crime, using either a corrupt verdict or brute force to satisfy the hatred of the moment [c1216]. Religion was honoured by neither side; only the use of fair phrases to achieve guilty ends was esteemed [c1216]. The moderate citizens perished, caught between the extremes [c1216]. In this environment, the old simplicity that valued honour was laughed down and vanished; society split into camps in which no one trusted another, no promise was kept, and no oath commanded respect [c1216]. Because men believed no permanent settlement was possible, they acted only for self-defence, and the more straightforward minds, fearing the cleverness of their opponents, resorted directly to violence, while their arrogant adversaries often fell through over-caution [c1216].

results.question: How does Thucydides contrast the Athenian and Spartan national characters?
results.ideal_answer: Largely through speeches. The Corinthians tell Sparta that the Athenians are addicted to innovation, swift in conception and execution, adventurous beyond their power, never at home — "born into the world to take no rest themselves and to give none to others" — while the Spartans keep what they have, invent nothing, and never go far enough. Archidamus answers by defending Spartan slowness as disciplined wisdom: warlike from self-control and honor, wise because educated too little to despise the laws.
results.answer:
Thucydides contrasts Athenian and Spartan national characters most extensively through the speech of the Corinthians at Sparta (Book 1), who declare that they will point out "the great contrast between the two national characters" [1]. The Corinthians list a series of paired opposites: the Athenians are "addicted to innovation" and swift in both planning and acting, while the Spartans have "a genius for keeping what you have got, accompanied by a total want of invention" [1]. Athenians are adventurous beyond their strength and optimistic in danger; Spartans attempt less than their strength allows and mistrust even their own judgment [1]. Where Athens shows "promptitude," Sparta shows "procrastination"; Athenians are "never at home," always abroad seeking gain, while Spartans are "never from it," fearing to endanger what they leave behind [1]. Athenians are quick to exploit success and slow to retreat from failure; they expend their bodies freely for the state and reserve their minds for its service, regarding any unexecuted plan as a loss and any success as only a partial achievement [1]. Their only holiday is to do what is needed, and in sum they "were born into the world to take no rest themselves and to give none to others" [1][2].

In his funeral oration, Pericles describes Athenian character partly by implicit contrast with Sparta. He notes that while the Spartans "from their very cradles by a painful discipline seek after manliness," Athenians live as they please yet meet danger with equal courage [4]. Athens opens its city to the world, trusting in native spirit rather than systems and exclusion, whereas Sparta restricts foreigners [4]. Athenians unite daring with deliberation in the same persons, cultivating refinement without extravagance and knowledge without softness [4][3].

Finally, in his own voice (Book 8) Thucydides comments that "the slowness and want of energy of the Lacedaemonians as contrasted with the dash and enterprise of their opponents" proved useful to Athens' maritime empire, and adds that the Syracusans "were most like the Athenians in character, and also most successful in combating them" [5].

results.question: What does Herodotus find most remarkable about Babylon?
results.ideal_answer: Its unmatched magnificence and its customs. He marvels at the city's scale — the deep moat and walls fifty royal cubits thick and two hundred high, and the temple of Zeus Belos with its eight stacked towers — and singles out two customs: the annual bride auction, where money from the beautiful maidens dowered the plain ones (in his opinion their wisest), and the carrying of the sick into the marketplace, since they use no physicians and every passer-by must offer advice.

results.question: How does Suetonius chart Caligula's descent from popular prince to monster?
results.ideal_answer: Suetonius structures the Life around an explicit pivot: "Thus far we have spoken of him as a prince. What remains to be said of him, bespeaks him rather a monster than a man." His accession brought genuine joy — the soldiers' beloved camp-child, heir to Germanicus' virtues — though Tiberius had already predicted he was "rearing a hydra for the people of Rome." After the pivot comes the monster: claiming divine majesty, beheading the gods' statues to mount his own head, the temple to his own divinity, and cruelties without limit.
results.answer:
Suetonius constructs Caligula’s biography as a dramatic fall, explicitly dividing it in Chapter XXII: “Thus far we have spoken of him as a prince. What remains to be said of him, bespeaks him rather a monster than a man” [23]. 

**The Popular Prince (Chapters XIII–XXII)**  
Suetonius first paints a picture of universal joy at Caligula’s accession. The Roman people, the soldiers who had known him as a child, and the provincials all hailed him with extravagant endearments — calling him “their star,” “their chick,” “their pretty puppet” [1]; over 160,000 victims were offered in sacrifice in the first three months [28]. Caligula actively fanned this affection: he delivered a tearful funeral speech for Tiberius, retrieved the ashes of his mother and brother with great ceremony, honored his grandmother Antonia, adopted his brother, restored exiles, and granted an amnesty for past offences [4]. 

**The Turning Point: Illness**  
Suetonius marks a physical and mental crisis early in the reign. “When he fell ill, the people hung about the Palatium all night long; some vowed … to risk their lives … for his recovery” [28]. The biographer later returns to this, suggesting that Caligula never recovered his sanity — whether from the illness itself, a love-potion given by his wife Caesonia, or “want of sleep” and “falling sickness” [6][2]. 

**The Monster (Chapters XXIII onwards)**  
After the pivot at XXII, Suetonius catalogues a relentless catalogue of horrors:  
- **Cruelty to family and senators**: he killed his brother Tiberius, forced his father-in-law Silanus to cut his throat, mistreated his grandmothers, and had senators run beside his litter or wait at his couch [11][21].  
- **Savage executions**: criminals were fed to wild beasts; knights were branded, sawn asunder, or shut in cages; parents were forced to watch their sons die [18].  
- **Capricious sadism**: he threw spectators into the sea, cut off a slave’s hands and displayed them, stabbed a gladiator with a dagger and paraded as a victor, and joked that he could have the consuls’ throats cut with a nod [12][16].  
- **Megalomania and extravagance**: he demanded divine worship, set up a gold statue of himself, rolled naked in heaps of gold coins, and lavished a marble stable, ivory manger, and plans for the consulship on his horse Incitatus [22][20].  
- **Profaning the gods**: he removed the heads from famous statues of the gods to replace them with his own, and whispered threats to Jupiter Capitolinus [23].  

Thus Suetonius charts the descent not as a gradual corruption but as a clean break: a beloved young prince, weakened and perhaps unhinged by a serious illness, who then reveals the monster that had always lurked beneath his obsequious earlier mask [14].

results.question: How does Tacitus characterize German marriage and the position of women?
results.ideal_answer: With pointed admiration. The marriage gifts — oxen, a bridled horse, shield, spear, and sword — admonish the wife that she comes as a partner in toils and dangers, "to suffer and to dare equally with him, in peace and in war... thus she is to live; thus to die." The Germans live "fenced around with chastity": adultery is extremely rare and instantly punished, no one calls seduction the way of the world, and in the strictest tribes a woman takes one husband "as one body and one life."

results.question: How does Suetonius portray the character of Tiberius?
results.ideal_answer: As a dissimulating tyrant whose vices, long imperfectly concealed, flowered once he withdrew to Capri. Even Augustus reportedly pitied the Roman people for being "ground by the jaws of such a slow devourer." The soldiers' nickname Biberius marked his drunkenness from youth; at Capri, "remote from the observation of the people of Rome, he abandoned himself to all the vicious propensities which he had long but imperfectly concealed"; and the translator's verdict is of an "execrable tyrant" whose indiscriminate cruelty spared neither age nor sex.
results.answer:
Suetonius portrays Tiberius as a deeply contradictory and ultimately monstrous figure, whose reign degenerated from a facade of moderation into unrestrained cruelty and depravity.

In the early part of his reign, Tiberius carefully cultivated an image of humility and restraint. He accepted only modest honours, refused the title of 'Father of his Country,' forbade temples and statues in his honour without permission, and showed an aversion to flattery, even reprimanding those who addressed him as 'lord' [13][11]. He assisted magistrates as a mere assessor and rose to greet spectators at public games, winning considerable public affection [12].

However, Suetonius makes clear that this moderation was largely a pretense. The characteristics 'most predominant in him were fear and distrust' [1]. He never attended a banquet without a guard of spearmen, had visitors searched, and was so timorous that a threatening letter from a conspirator nearly made him abdicate [1].

After the death of his son Drusus, and especially following the fall of Sejanus, Tiberius abandoned all restraint and 'abandoned himself to every species of cruelty' [3]. Suetonius provides a litany of horrors: not a day passed without a punishment, even on holidays; wives and children shared the fate of the condemned; a poet was tried for abusing Agamemnon, and a historian for calling Brutus and Cassius 'the last of the Romans' [3]. At Capri, he had victims tortured and thrown from a cliff before his eyes, their bones broken by sailors waiting below [2]. He invented new tortures, such as forcing men to drink heavily and then tying their members with harp-strings to stop their urine [2].

Suetonius also emphasizes Tiberius's sexual depravity, particularly during his retreat to Capri. There he created an apartment for 'abominable lewdness,' assembling 'inventors of unnatural copulations' called Spintriae, and furnished rooms with pornographic paintings and books so that no one might 'want a pattern for the execution of any lewd project' [15]. He had young persons of both sexes prostitute themselves in woods and groves disguised as Pans and Nymphs [15]. He was said to have abused a youth during a sacrifice and later broke the legs of the boy and his brother for upbraiding each other [17]. A woman named Mallonia stabbed herself rather than submit to his lust, openly upbraiding the 'vile old lecher' [16].

His vices extended to gluttony and drunkenness. Even as a young soldier he was nicknamed 'Biberius' for his excessive drinking [6]. As emperor, he spent a whole night and two days feasting with companions, and promoted a man for drinking an amphora of wine at a single draught [6]. He would eat and drink at any time or place, often to the point of intoxication, and would then fall asleep on his back with his mouth open, requiring a feather to be put down his throat to make him vomit [7].

Suetonius also notes his complete lack of paternal affection. He was little moved by the death of his own son Drusus, and actively disparaged his adopted son Germanicus, railing at his victories as ruinous to the state and being widely suspected of having him poisoned [4].

In a summarizing passage, Suetonius writes that Tiberius 'seems to have conducted himself with a uniform repugnance to nature,' indulging from his earliest years 'a moroseness of disposition, which counterfeited the appearance of austere virtue,' and in his later years launching 'forth into excesses, of a kind the most unnatural and most detestable' [14]. The only checks on his behaviour were a 'filial awe' of his mother Livia and a 'slavish fear of Sejanus'; once both were removed, 'he rioted without any control' [14].

results.question: How does Josephus portray the factional strife among the Jews that doomed Jerusalem?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus presents the fall of Jerusalem as a self-inflicted catastrophe of civil war within the walls. The zealots fractured into rival factions — Eleazar son of Simon making "the first separation of the zealots from the people" and seizing the inner temple, against John of Gischala and, in the city, Simon — until there were "three treacherous factions" fighting one another even as Titus besieged them. In that warfare they burned the storehouses, so that almost all the corn "which would have been sufficient for a siege of many years" was destroyed and the people were "taken by the means of the famine" their own leaders had prepared. The common people were "their prey on both sides," and Josephus moralizes that no age ever bred "a generation more fruitful in wickedness" — the Jews destroyed themselves.
results.answer:
Josephus portrays the factional strife that doomed Jerusalem as the true cause of the city's destruction — worse than the Roman siege itself — and as a manifestation of divine justice against a nation consumed by its own wickedness.

**The Three Factions and Their Mutual Destruction**

Josephus describes how the sedition within Jerusalem broke into three factions, each fighting the others. Eleazar held the inner temple, John of Gischala controlled the outer temple and parts of the lower city, and Simon son of Gioras held the upper city [2]. They fought one another relentlessly, even while the Romans were encamped at the walls [4]. John used engines of war against the temple above him, slaughtering priests as they performed their sacred ministrations [8]. The three factions burned the houses and food stores between them, creating a desolate no-man's-land — and in doing so, Josephus says, they "cut off the nerves of their own power" and destroyed provisions that could have sustained a siege for years [2].

**The Sedition as the True Destroyer of the City**

Josephus's central thesis is explicit: "I venture to affirm that the sedition destroyed the city, and the Romans destroyed the sedition, which it was a much harder thing to do than to destroy the walls; so that we may justly ascribe our misfortunes to our own people, and the just vengeance taken on them to the Romans" [4]. He calls the sedition "a sedition begotten by another sedition, and to be like a wild beast grown mad, which, for want of food from abroad, fell now upon eating its own flesh" [1]. The people trapped between the factions were "like a great body torn in pieces," and the aged and women "wished for the Romans, and earnestly hoped for an external war, in order to their delivery from their domestical miseries" [2].

**Divine Justice and Providence**

Throughout, Josephus frames the internal strife as God's judgment. He says the partition into three factions "may be said to be a good thing, and the effect of Divine justice" [1]. He has himself declaring to the Jews that "it is God himself who is bringing on this fire, to purge that city and temple by means of the Romans" [10]. The Zealots and Idumeans "all met with such ends as God deservedly brought upon them in way of punishment" [14].

**The Character of the Zealots**

Josephus heaps scorn on the Zealots, whose very name he treats as ironic: "they gave themselves that name from their zeal for what was good, yet did it agree to them only by way of irony, on account of those they had unjustly treated by their wild and brutish disposition, or as thinking the greatest mischiefs to be the greatest good" [14]. They polluted the temple with their blood, cut the throats of the high priests, "destroyed utterly the least remains of a political government, and introduced the most complete scene of iniquity" [14]. The high priests urged the people to "purge the temple of these bloody polluters of it" [24].

**Wider Civil Discord and Atrocities**

Even before the siege, Josephus reports that "disorders and civil wars" raged in every city; those at peace with the Romans "turned their hands one against another" — families, friends, and communities split into warring factions [3]. During the famine, the robbers among the seditious broke into houses that were "no other than graves of dead bodies," plundered the dead, and even stabbed the dying, while those perishing "died with their eyes fixed upon the temple, and left the seditious alive behind them" [21]. The horrors culminated in the infamous case of Mary of Bethezob, who ate her own child — an act Josephus recounts in grim detail [17], and which Titus himself blamed on the Jews' choice of "sedition" over "peace" [19].

**The Root Cause in Josephus's Wider View**

In his *Antiquities*, Josephus traces the destruction further back to the "fourth philosophic sect" founded by Judas and Sadduc, which "filled our civil government with tumults" and "laid the foundations of our future miseries" — the infection of which "spread thence among the younger sort, who were zealous for it, brought the public to destruction" [15].

Throughout, Josephus presents the factional strife not merely as a political disaster but as a moral and theological catastrophe: a nation so consumed by internal hatred and impiety that it destroyed itself before the Romans ever breached the walls.

results.question: How does Josephus characterize the reign of Herod the Great?
results.ideal_answer: Josephus draws Herod as a king of dazzling success abroad and monstrous cruelty at home. He stood so high in Rome's favour that "he arrived at that pitch of felicity" of being, after Agrippa, the man Caesar valued most, and he crowned his reign with vast building works, "undertaking a very great work, that is, to build of himself the temple of God," alongside Caesarea and his fortresses. Yet the suspicion that secured his throne consumed his own household: he had his beloved wife tried and executed — "thus was Mariamne led to execution" — and later his sons. His end matched his cruelty: a loathsome disease in which "his privy-member was putrefied, and produced worms," which Josephus reads as divine punishment.
results.answer:
Josephus presents a deeply ambivalent but psychologically unified portrait of Herod the Great. In the most important summary passage (Antiquities), he acknowledges that observers are astonished by the “diversity of Herod's nature and purposes”: when one considers his magnificence and the benefits he bestowed on mankind, even his detractors must confess he had “a nature vastly beneficent”; yet when one looks at the punishments he inflicted and the injuries he did to his subjects and his nearest relations, one is forced to allow that he was “brutish, and a stranger to all humanity” [1]. Josephus, however, rejects the idea that Herod had a self-contradictory nature. He argues that a single cause produced both sets of actions: Herod was “a man ambitious of honor, and quite overcome by that passion.” His ambition drove him to be magnificent wherever it promised a lasting memorial or present reputation; but because his expenditures exceeded his revenues, “he was necessitated to be harsh to his subjects.” Conscious of being hated for his injuries, and finding it inconvenient for his revenue to amend his offences, he strove to turn their ill-will into a source of gain. Even within his own court, anyone who was not perfectly obsequious or who seemed to contemplate innovation was prosecuted — even kindred and friends — “out of a desire that he might be himself alone honored” [1].

This dual character is fleshed out throughout both the *Antiquities* and the *Wars*. Herod kept his people obedient “as well by the fear they stood in of him, for he was implacable in the infliction of his punishments, as by the provident care he had showed towards them” in their distresses [5]. He cultivated the favor of Roman grandees through magnificent liberality, but his building of cities and temples outside Judea transgressed Jewish customs — though he excused it as done to please Caesar and the Romans, Josephus says he “had himself entirely in view all the while, and indeed was very ambitious to leave great monuments of his government to posterity” [5].

Those monuments were truly vast. In the *Wars*, Josephus catalogues them in detail: Herod named cities after his father (Antipatris), mother (Cypros), and brother (Phasaelus); built the fortress-palace Herodium on an artificial hill with marble steps, round towers, and lavish palaces; and bestowed palaces, aqueducts, baths, temples, theatres, market-places, gymnasia, and annual revenues on cities throughout the eastern Mediterranean — Tripoli, Damascus, Tyre, Sidon, Laodicea, Ascalon, Rhodes, Athens, Sparta, Pergamum, Antioch, and many others [9][10]. Josephus remarks that “the fear he was in much disturbed the greatness of his soul, lest he should be exposed to envy” for his excessive liberality [6]. He also notes Herod's physical excellence — a body suited to his soul, an outstanding hunter and horseman who could catch forty wild beasts in a day, a warrior who could not be withstood, and a man favored by fortune so that he seldom failed in war except through betrayal or his soldiers' rashness [6].

Yet the dark side was ever-present. Herod's jealous love for his wife Mariamne, stoked by his sister Salome's calumnies, led him to have her executed after a hasty and impassioned trial [12]. His sons by Mariamne, Alexander and Aristobulus, were steadily undermined by the intrigues of his eldest son Antipater, who fed Herod's suspicions until the king “was already become very ill-disposed towards the young men” and eventually sailed to Rome to accuse them before Caesar [15][13]. The *Wars* records Alexander's threat to expose before Caesar “the calamities of their nation, and how they are taxed to death, and in what ways of luxury and wicked practices that wealth is spent which was gotten by bloodshed” — a bitter summary of the tyranny that shadowed Herod's magnificence [17].

In sum, Josephus characterizes Herod's reign as that of a man consumed by the passion for honor: a ruler who lavished extraordinary benefactions on his subjects and on foreign cities, yet who was at the same time suspicious, implacably cruel, and ultimately a tyrant to his own family and people — both aspects flowing, in Josephus's analysis, from the same insatiable ambition.

results.question: How does Tacitus portray the role of the armies in making and unmaking emperors in the civil war?
results.ideal_answer: Tacitus makes the legions the true power in the state. The convulsion of 69 laid bare "that secret of the empire, that emperors could be made elsewhere than at Rome" — sovereignty now belonged to whichever army would proclaim its general. That loyalty was bought: the city soldiery turned on Nero and were inclined to change "when they found that the donative promised in Galba's name was withheld," and under each claimant discipline dissolved — "Much however as Vitellius indulged his generals, his soldiers enjoyed yet greater licence." The praetorians embodied the menace, bursting in arms upon Otho's banquet and threatening the Senate. Throughout, emperors are made and unmade by the greed, licence, and violence of the soldiery.

results.question: How does Xenophon portray the rule and downfall of the Thirty Tyrants at Athens?
results.ideal_answer: Xenophon shows the Thirty beginning with popular approval and ending in bloody tyranny and overthrow. At first they prosecuted the informers and base men of the democracy, and "the new senate gladly recorded its vote of condemnation against them"; but they soon turned to plunder and murder, passing a resolution "empowering each of the committee to seize on one of the resident aliens apiece, to put his victim to death, and to confiscate his property." Their rule grew so murderous that in eight months they had "slain almost more men than the Peloponnesians together in ten years of warfare." When Theramenes resisted Critias he was struck off the roll and "dragged through the Agora" to his death. Finally Thrasybulus and the exiles from Phyle prevailed, and the Athenians "reappointed the several magistrates; the constitution began to work afresh, and civic life was recommenced."
results.answer:
Xenophon's *Hellenica* (Book II) provides the most detailed surviving contemporary account of the Thirty Tyrants at Athens. His portrayal is unsparingly negative, tracing an arc from constitutional pretence through escalating bloodshed and internal conflict to military defeat and deposition.

**Establishment and initial moderation**
After Athens' surrender, the Thirty were tasked with compiling ancestral laws, but they instead appointed a compliant senate and magistrates as they saw fit [4]. At first they targeted notorious informers (sycophants) who had plagued respectable citizens under the democracy; the new senate gladly condemned them, and the general populace, feeling no kinship with the victims, was not yet alarmed [4].

**Escalation: Critias versus Theramenes**
Xenophon traces the regime's radicalisation through the personal rivalry between the moderate Theramenes and the ruthless Critias. While Critias thirsted for the blood of the democracy that had exiled him, Theramenes protested that men who had done no wrong were being executed merely for their past influence [3]. Critias retorted that, since the Thirty intended "to take the lion's share," their government required as careful guarding as a tyranny, and that the list of the condemned grew daily [3]. Fearing Theramenes was becoming a popular idol, the Thirty drew up a list of Three Thousand citizens who were to share in government; Theramenes found it ridiculous, arguing that they were creating a government based on force yet weaker than those it sought to govern [5]. During a military review, the rest of the citizens were disarmed by the Spartan garrison and the Thirty's partisans, leaving only the Three Thousand armed [5].

**Wholesale butchery and the execution of Theramenes**
With the city disarmed, the Thirty embarked on "wholesale butchery, to which many were sacrificed to the merest hatred, many to the accident of possessing riches" [1]. To pay their guards, each member of the committee was empowered to seize one resident alien, put him to death, and confiscate his property. Theramenes refused to participate, calling it an injustice worse than that of the sycophants [1].

Critias thereupon plotted against him. He packed the senate house with young men carrying concealed daggers and with foreign guards, then struck Theramenes from the list of the Three Thousand and condemned him to death [2]. Though Theramenes clung to the altar of Hestia and called on gods and men to witness the outrage, he was dragged through the Agora by Satyrus. Xenophon records two famous apophthegms: when Satyrus told him to be silent or he would rue the day, Theramenes replied, "And if I be silent, shall I not rue it?"; and when he drank the hemlock, he playfully dashed out the dregs saying, "This to the lovely Critias"—a display of composure that Xenophon deems admirable [8].

**The tyranny after Theramenes**
With their last internal critic removed, the Thirty "felt that they had it in their power to play the tyrant without fear" [6]. They forbade all whose names were not on the list from entering the city; even the countryside and Piraeus offered no safety, and Megara and Thebes overflowed with refugees [6]. They also seized Eleusis, summoning its citizens to a registration, then arresting each man as he emerged, and compelling the Three Thousand and Knights to vote for their mass condemnation in the presence of armed Laconian guards [7].

**Downfall and deposition**
Thrasybulus with about seventy followers seized the fortress of Phyle; the Thirty's attempt to blockade it was thwarted by heavy snow [6]. After subsequent skirmishes, the exiles in Piraeus grew numerous enough to take the field, while the city faction descended into mutual distrust [13]. In the final confrontation near Piraeus, the Thirty were defeated; Critias himself, Hippomachus, and Charmides were among the seventy dead [9]. The herald Cleocritus made an impassioned plea to the city party to cease obeying "these most unhallowed Thirty, who for the sake of private gain have in eight months slain almost more men than the Peloponnesians together in ten years of warfare" [9][10].

The next day, the Thirty sat in deep desolation, and the Three Thousand voted to depose them, electing a board of Ten (one per tribe) in their place. The Thirty themselves retired to Eleusis [10][13]. Peace was ultimately brokered by the Spartan king Pausanias, on terms that allowed the exiles to return home while the Thirty, the Eleven, and the Ten governors of Piraeus were excepted; those of the city party who still feared reprisals were permitted to settle at Eleusis [11].

Throughout his narrative, Xenophon portrays the Thirty not merely as harsh oligarchs but as men driven by greed and fear into outright tyranny—systematically disarming the populace, executing opponents without trial, plundering wealth, and ultimately provoking a civil war that consumed the regime's own leaders.

results.question: How does Strabo portray the decayed condition of the once-great cities of Greece in his own day?
results.ideal_answer: Strabo repeatedly registers how the once-great cities of Greece had dwindled or vanished by his own day. In the Argolid "some of the cities mentioned in the Catalogue of the Ships, and said to be subject to Argos, have disappeared." In Arcadia the famous cities — Mantinea, Orchomenus, Heraea, Cleitor, Pheneus, Stymphalus and the rest — "either exist no longer, or traces and signs only of their existence are visible," the country sunk into a deserted condition. Lesser towns such as Dyspontium "was razed, and the greatest part of the inhabitants removed" abroad. Even Corinth had been sacked, Strabo recalling Polybius's eyewitness account of the soldiers' indifference to its art and sacred offerings. The recurring note is depopulation and ruin set against a glorious past.

results.question: How does Polybius explain the natural cycle by which forms of government rise and degenerate?
results.ideal_answer: In Book VI Polybius lays out the natural cycle (anacyclosis) by which constitutions rise and decay. Society begins under the rule of the strongest, but once a leader rewards merit and protects the weak, men "no longer obey him and support his rule from fear of violence, but rather from conviction of its utility" — force matures into legitimate kingship. Each good form then breeds its corrupt twin: kingship into tyranny, aristocracy into oligarchy, and democracy into mob-violence, where "tumultuous assemblies, massacres, banishments, redivisions of land" reduce the state until "it has once more found a master and a despot." "This is the regular cycle of constitutional revolutions." The escape is the mixed constitution: "Lycurgus... combined together all the excellences and distinctive features of the best constitutions, that no part should become unduly predominant" — the balance Polybius finds perfected at Rome.
results.answer: Polybius explains the natural cycle of governments (anacyclosis) as an inevitable process rooted in human nature. He identifies six forms: three good forms—kingship, aristocracy, democracy—and their three corrupt counterparts—tyranny, oligarchy, mob-rule (ochlocracy) [2]. The cycle begins with a primitive, natural monarchy or despotism, which through reason and adjustment evolves into kingship [2]. However, kingship degenerates into tyranny when rulers indulge their appetites, provoking the noblest citizens to overthrow it [1][2]. Out of gratitude, the people entrust power to these leaders, giving rise to aristocracy [1]. But when the sons of the aristocrats, unfamiliar with hardship, pursue greed and licentiousness, aristocracy turns into oligarchy [1]. The people then rise up, expel the oligarchs, and establish a democracy, valuing equality and freedom [4]. Yet as new generations lose sight of these values, the rich seek power, corrupt the populace with bribes, and democracy degenerates into mob-rule (ochlocracy), characterized by violence, confiscations, and lawlessness [4][2][3]. Ultimately, this chaos produces a master or despot, and the cycle returns to its original stage [3]. Polybius emphasizes that these changes follow an 'undeviating law of nature,' just as iron rusts and timber decays from within, because each form of government contains an inherent vice that inevitably destroys it [3].

results.question: How does Polybius describe the organization and discipline of the Roman army?
results.ideal_answer: Polybius's Book VI admires the Roman army as a machine of organization and discipline. At the levy the military tribunes "muster their men" and bind each with an oath "that he will obey his officers," before sorting them into velites, hastati, principes and triarii. On campaign they entrench a standardized camp on one fixed plan, the general's Praetorium sited first and the streets laid out around it. Discipline is enforced by fear: a cohort that flees is decimated, the tribune selecting by lot "about a tenth of those who have been guilty of the act of cowardice" for the fustuarium; valour, by contrast, is so publicly rewarded that brave men become "marked men in all solemn festivals." Polybius caps the account by contrast — in land service "the Romans train themselves to a much higher pitch than the Carthaginians," who "wholly neglect their infantry."

results.question: How does Livy portray Hannibal as Rome's great antagonist across the Second Punic War?
results.ideal_answer: Livy makes Hannibal Rome's supreme antagonist, drawn double-edged from the first: matchless "high virtues of the hero" shadowed by "inhuman cruelty, more than Punic perfidy, no truth... no fear of the gods, no respect for oaths." He proves the virtues at once, bringing his army into Italy "in the fifth month... having crossed the Alps in fifteen days" through appalling loss. Yet Livy fixes the fatal flaw after Cannae, when Maharbal urges an instant march on Rome and, refused, retorts "You know how to conquer, Hannibal; but you do not know how to make use of your victory" — a delay "believed to have been the preservation of the city and the empire." The long Italian war winds to its end at Zama, where Hannibal, "after performing this as it were his last work of valour, fled to Adrumetum," and confessed in the senate "that he was defeated, not only in the battle, but in the war."
results.answer:
Livy portrays Hannibal as Rome's destined and most formidable antagonist across the Second Punic War by weaving together a complex picture of his character, achievements, and ultimate tragedy. Several key themes emerge from Livy's narrative.

**1. A hatred ordained from childhood.** Livy opens the war by recounting a story that Hannibal, at about nine years old, was brought by his father Hamilcar to an altar and made to swear an oath 'to prove himself, as soon as he could, an enemy to the Roman people' [26]. Hannibal himself recalls this oath decades later before King Antiochus: 'Under the obligation of this oath, I carried arms against them for thirty-six years' [32]. Livy thus frames Hannibal's entire career as the fulfillment of a sacred, lifelong vendetta.

**2. A character of towering virtues and monstrous vices.** Livy's famous character sketch (Book 21) presents Hannibal as a paradox. On the one hand, 'There never was a genius more fitted for the two most opposite duties of obeying and commanding'; he was fearless, prudent, immune to bodily exhaustion, indifferent to pleasure, and 'foremost to advance to the charge, was last to leave the engagement' [2]. On the other, 'Excessive vices counterbalanced these high virtues of the hero; inhuman cruelty, more than Punic perfidy, no truth, no reverence for things sacred, no fear of the gods, no respect for oaths, no sense of religion' [2]. Hanno the Carthaginian senator calls him 'the fury and firebrand of this war' [5].

**3. The author of Rome's greatest disasters.** Livy describes the war as 'the most memorable of all wars that were ever waged' [26]. Under Hannibal's generalship, the Romans suffer catastrophic defeats. At Lake Trasimene, 'Fifteen thousand Romans were slain,' and Hannibal, having promised safe passage to those who surrendered, instead 'threw them all into chains' — a breach 'kept by Hannibal with Punic fidelity' [10]. At Cannae, the disaster is even greater: 'Forty thousand foot, two thousand seven hundred horse... are said to have been slain,' a battle Livy judges 'more important and more horrible with respect to the slaughter of the army' than even the rout at the Allia [11]. The terror in Rome afterward was such that 'No other nation surely would not have been overwhelmed by such an accumulation of misfortune' [9].

**4. The menace at the gates.** Livy dramatizes Hannibal's march on Rome itself: 'Crossing the Anio, he pitched his camp three miles from the city, and lastly, came up to the very walls and gates' [1]. Scipio later recalls 'the Carthaginian camp pitched between the Anio and the walls of Rome, and the victorious Hannibal seen almost in our gates' [14]. The image of Hannibal physically threatening the city's walls epitomizes Livy's portrait of him as the ultimate existential threat.

**5. Cruelty and perfidy in action.** Livy repeatedly illustrates the vices announced in the character sketch. Hannibal burns alive the wife and children of a suspected defector, Altinius, 'after having made inquiry... touching the quantity of gold and silver which was left at his house' [6]. On leaving Italy, many Italian allies who refused to follow him to Africa and took refuge in the temple of Juno Lacinia 'were barbarously massacred in the very temple' — a shrine 'which had never been violated up to that day' [17]. Even his own supposed allies, the Locrians, were treated with 'such insolence and cruelty' by the Carthaginian garrison that Roman misrule seemed preferable [29].

**6. Decline and tragic departure.** Despite his triumphs, Livy presents Hannibal's later war as one of frustration and attrition. When the head of his brother Hasdrubal is thrown before his camp, Hannibal 'is said to have declared that he recognised the destiny of Carthage' [18]. His recall is presented as a deeply emotional undoing: 'It is said that when Hannibal heard the message... he gnashed with his teeth, groaned, and scarcely refrained from shedding tears' [17]. He declares himself 'conquered, not by the Roman people, who have been so often slain and routed, but by the Carthaginian senate, through envy and detraction' [17]. Livy notes that 'rarely any person leaving his country to go into exile exhibited deeper sorrow than Hannibal did on departing from the land of his enemies,' and that he 'cursed himself and his own head that he did not lead his troops, while reeking with blood from the victory at Cannae, to Rome' [17]. After the final defeat at Zama, Hannibal 'confessed in the senate-house that he was defeated, not only in the battle, but in the war' [20].

In sum, Livy builds Hannibal into a figure who is simultaneously admirable and monstrous, a divinely-sanctioned scourge whose own flawed nature and the envy of his countrymen bring about his fall. He is the enemy who brought Rome nearer to destruction than any other, and his long occupation of Italy — and his grief at leaving it — become the measure of Rome's ultimate triumph over him.
